# SOP-CLIN-005 vs. NIST AI RMF 1.0 Foundational Expectations — Detailed Assessment

## Overview

NIST AI RMF 1.0 (NIST AI 100-1, January 2023) establishes two layers of foundational expectations:

1. **Part 1 — Trustworthiness Characteristics** (§3): Valid & Reliable, Safe, Secure & Resilient, Accountable & Transparent, Explainable & Interpretable, Privacy-Enhanced, Fair with Harmful Bias Managed.
2. **Part 2 — Core Functions** (§5): GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE — each with categories and subcategories.

Below is a systematic evaluation of SOP-CLIN-005 against each.

---

## A. Trustworthiness Characteristics (NIST AI RMF §3, Fig. 4)

### 1. Valid & Reliable — ✅ Strong Coverage

NIST states that "Valid & Reliable is a necessary condition of trustworthiness and is shown as the base for other trustworthiness characteristics" (NIST AI 100-1, §3, Fig. 4). Validity means the system does what it is intended to do; reliability means it performs consistently and accurately.

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-specified performance thresholds | §5.1 Clinical Performance Protocol requires statistically derived, clinically justified minimums (Sensitivity 95% CI LB > 0.85, Specificity 95% CI LB > 0.80, ECE < 0.05) | ✅ |
| Independent validation | §5.4 Independent Clinical Validation by Clinical Validation Lead, separate from Core Development Team, on an external dataset | ✅ |
| Strict data separation | §5.2 Cryptographic patient-level hashing; three-way split (60/15/25); hash key in HashiCorp Vault inaccessible to developers | ✅ |
| Calibration as gate | §4.3 ECE > 0.05 = automatic failure; calibration curves reviewed and approved as release criterion | ✅ |
| Ongoing reliability monitoring | §7.2 Monthly clinical monitoring (200-case sample, ground truth adjudication, metric trending vs. baseline) | ✅ |

### 2. Safe — ✅ Strong Coverage

NIST §3.2: AI systems should "not, under defined conditions, cause physical or psychological harm" and should "fail safely, particularly if made to operate beyond its knowledge limits."

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-deployment safety gate | §4.1 No deployment without CMO + VP Clinical AI release authorization | ✅ |
| Human control / advisory-only | §4.7 "The licensed healthcare professional at the point of care remains wholly and solely responsible" | ✅ |
| Safe failure mode | §6.1 TC-02 Low-confidence outputs suppressed with `LOW_CONFIDENCE` status and fallback message | ✅ |
| Input validation | §6.1 TC-01 Invalid input returns `INPUT_VALIDATION_ERROR` instead of erroneous prediction | ✅ |
| Human factors evaluation | §5.5 Validates time-to-decision and cognitive error rate in simulated clinical environment | ✅ |
| Phased rollout | §5.7 Mandatory 14-day Silent Mode before clinical display | ✅ |

### 3. Secure & Resilient — ⚠️ Partial

NIST §3.3: Security involves protection against attacks; resilience involves the ability to recover from errors or disruptions.

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Adversarial input protection | §6.1 TC-04 Adversarial Input Filter screens for anomalous patterns | ✅ |
| Access control | §6.2 Production model weights restricted to MLOps Deployment Team | ✅ |
| Audit trail | §6.1 TC-05 Immutable audit log with inference ID, hashed patient ID, model version, timestamps | ✅ |
| Comprehensive security testing | **Not addressed** — no penetration testing, red-teaming, or model extraction/theft prevention | ❌ |
| Incident response for security events | **Not addressed** — §7.1 references ITIL incident management for performance alerts but not security-specific response | ❌ |
| Encryption / data protection in transit and at rest | **Not addressed** in this SOP | ❌ |

> **Gap:** Security controls are present at the input/output boundary but the SOP lacks comprehensive security evaluation, adversarial robustness testing, and incident response procedures. These may exist in SOP-SOFT-002 or SOP-MLOPS-001 but are not in this document.

### 4. Accountable & Transparent — ✅ Strong Coverage

NIST §3.4: "Accountability and transparency also relate to the processes and activities internal to an AI system and its external setting" (NIST AI 100-1, §3).

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Clear accountability | §3 RACI matrix with no single individual holding contradictory roles | ✅ |
| Executive responsibility | §3 VP Clinical AI (Accountable) and CMO (Approver) for release | ✅ |
| Transparent release package | §5.6 Version-controlled bundle in Greenlight QMS with all validation reports | ✅ |
| Audit logging | §6.1 TC-05 Every inference logged immutably | ✅ |
| Exception transparency | §10 All deviations documented with risk assessment, compensatory controls, and expiry dates | ✅ |
| Periodic reporting | §7.3 Quarterly Business Review to CMO and CTO | ✅ |

### 5. Explainable & Interpretable — ⚠️ Partial

NIST §3.5: Explainability relates to "the mechanisms of AI systems and how they produce outputs"; interpretability relates to "the meaning of AI system outputs."

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Output interpretability | §4.5 Confidence scores in natural language or intuitive visual formats (e.g., "Based on a cohort of N similar patients…") | ✅ |
| Clinically actionable phrasing | §5.5 Defines UI presentation with confidence intervals and actionable phrasing | ✅ |
| Model-level explainability (how the model reasons) | **Not addressed** — no requirement for SHAP, LIME, attention maps, feature importance, or other model-level explanation techniques | ❌ |
| Documentation of model logic | **Not addressed** — no requirement for model cards, algorithmic impact assessments, or documentation of internal reasoning | ❌ |

> **Gap:** The SOP addresses *output-level* interpretability well but does not require *model-level* explainability — understanding *why* the model produces a given output. For diagnostic AI, this is a significant gap per NIST's emphasis on both explainability and interpretability.

### 6. Privacy-Enhanced — ⚠️ Partial

NIST §3.6: Privacy-enhanced refers to "respecting privacy during AI system design, development, and deployment."

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| HIPAA-compliant logging | §6.1 TC-05 Audit logs are HIPAA-compliant with hashed patient IDs | ✅ |
| Reference to data governance | §1.2 and §11.1 reference SOP-DATA-001 (Clinical Data Integrity and Management) | ✅ (indirect) |
| Privacy-by-design / data minimization | **Not addressed** in this SOP | ❌ |
| Differential privacy / de-identification | **Not addressed** in this SOP | ❌ |
| Consent management | **Not addressed** in this SOP | ❌ |

> **Gap:** Privacy controls are deferred entirely to SOP-DATA-001. While cross-SOP referencing is reasonable, NIST expects privacy considerations to be integrated into the AI risk management process itself (GOV 1.2, MEASURE 2.13).

### 7. Fair — with Harmful Bias Managed — ✅ Strong Coverage

NIST §3.7: AI systems should be evaluated for differential performance across subgroups.

| NIST Expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 Provision | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Mandatory subgroup analysis | §4.4 Subgroup Analysis Report is mandatory for every release | ✅ |
| Pre-specified subgroups | §5.1 Subgroups defined in Clinical Performance Protocol | ✅ |
| Statistical and clinical significance testing | §5.8 Flags subgroups with p<0.05 or >5% absolute difference in sensitivity/specificity | ✅ |
| Root-cause analysis and mitigation | §5.8 Requires investigational root-cause analysis and actionable mitigation plan (label clarification, use restriction, re-training) | ✅ |
| Post-market subgroup surveillance | §7.3 QBR includes aggregated subgroup findings from post-market surveillance | ✅ |

---

## B. Core Functions (NIST AI RMF §5)

### GOVERN (§5.1) — ⚠️ Partial

| Subcategory | Assessment | Detail |
|---|---|---|
| GOV 1.1 (Legal/regulatory requirements) | ✅ | §1.2 references FDA Class II/III, EU AI Act Annex III; §11.2 references ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 62304, GMLP |
| GOV 1.2 (Trustworthy characteristics integrated) | ⚠️ | Validity, safety, fairness, transparency, human control are integrated; security and privacy are not deeply addressed |
| GOV 1.3 (Risk management based on risk tolerance) | ✅ | §5.1 pre-specified thresholds; §10 risk-based exception approval |
| GOV 1.4 (Transparent policies, controls) | ✅ | Version-controlled in Greenlight QMS |
| GOV 1.5 (Ongoing monitoring, periodic review) | ✅ | §7.1 real-time, §7.2 monthly, §7.3 quarterly |
| **GOV 1.6 (AI system inventory)** | **❌** | **No mention of an AI system inventory or registry** |
| **GOV 1.7 (Decommissioning)** | **❌** | **Mentioned in scope (§1.2) but no decommissioning procedure is defined** |
| GOV 2.1 (Roles documented) | ✅ | §3 RACI matrix |
| GOV 2.2 (Training) | ✅ | §9 Four training modules with 100% pass rate requirement |
| GOV 2.3 (Executive responsibility) | ✅ | VP Clinical AI and CMO accountability |
| GOV 3.1 (Diverse team) | ⚠️ | Medical Advisory Board has "diverse geographic and clinical settings" but no explicit demographic or disciplinary diversity requirement for core teams |
| GOV 3.2 (Human-AI oversight policies) | ✅ | §4.7, §5.5 |
| GOV 4.1 (Safety-first mindset) | ✅ | §4.1 Pre-Clinical Hold, §4.7 Human Control |
| GOV 4.2 (Document and communicate risks) | ✅ | §5.6, §7.3, §10 |
| GOV 4.3 (Practices for testing, error identification) | ✅ | §5.3, §5.4, §6.1 TC-01–TC-05 |

### MAP (§5.2) — ✅ Mostly Covered

| Subcategory | Assessment | Detail |
|---|---|---|
| MAP 1 (Context understood) | ✅ | §5.1 Clinical Performance Protocol defines intended use, population, workflow |
| MAP 2 (Categorization) | ✅ | §1.2 Classifies as FDA Class II/III, EU AI Act high-risk |
| MAP 3 (Capabilities understood) | ✅ | §5.1, §5.5 define capabilities, limitations, human oversight |
| MAP 4 (Third-party risks) | ⚠️ | §11.1 references related SOPs but no direct mapping of third-party component risks |
| MAP 5 (Impacts characterized) | ⚠️ | §5.8 characterizes subgroup impacts; broader societal impact assessment not explicitly required |

### MEASURE (§5.3) — ✅ Strong, with Gaps

| Subcategory | Assessment | Detail |
|---|---|---|
| MEASURE 1.1 (Methods and metrics identified) | ✅ | §5.3, §5.4 define comprehensive metrics with CIs |
| MEASURE 1.2 (Metrics regularly assessed) | ✅ | §7.2 monthly monitoring, §6.2 calibration audits |
| MEASURE 1.3 (Independent review) | ✅ | §5.4 Clinical Validation Lead is independent; §6.2 peer review of code |
| MEASURE 2.1–2.6 | ✅ | Test sets documented, representative data, deployment-like conditions (Silent Mode), production monitoring, validity demonstrated, safety evaluated |
| MEASURE 2.7 (Security evaluation) | ❌ | No comprehensive security evaluation |
| MEASURE 2.8–2.12 (Transparency, fairness, human-AI) | ✅ | Audit logs, subgroup analysis, human factors |
| MEASURE 2.13 (Privacy evaluation) | ❌ | Not addressed in this SOP |
| MEASURE 3.1 (Risk tracking) | ✅ | §7.1, §7.2 |
| **MEASURE 3.3 (End-user feedback/appeal)** | **❌** | **No mechanism for end users or affected individuals to report problems or appeal AI outcomes** |
| MEASURE 4.1–4.3 (Measurement feedback) | ⚠️ | Domain experts (MDs) involved in monitoring, but no consultation with affected communities about measurement approaches |

### MANAGE (§5.4) — ⚠️ Partial

| Subcategory | Assessment | Detail |
|---|---|---|
| MANAGE 1.1 (Proceed/stop decision) | ✅ | §5.6 Release Authorization |
| MANAGE 1.2 (Risk prioritization) | ✅ | §10 risk-based approval matrix (Minor/Major/Critical) |
| MANAGE 1.3 (Risk response) | ✅ | §10 exception process with compensatory controls |
| MANAGE 1.4 (Residual risk documentation) | ⚠️ | Documented for exceptions only, not systematically for all models |
| **MANAGE 2.1 (Non-AI alternatives)** | **❌** | **No consideration of viable non-AI alternative systems** |
| MANAGE 2.2 (Sustain deployed value) | ✅ | §7.1, §7.2 continuous monitoring |
| MANAGE 2.3 (Emergent risk response) | ⚠️ | §10 exception process partially covers this; no explicit procedure for previously unknown risks outside exceptions |
| MANAGE 2.4 (Incident response) | ✅ | §7.1 critical alerts trigger ITIL incident management |
| **MANAGE 3.1 (Risk communication to stakeholders)** | **⚠️** | Internal communication (QBR) is strong; **no communication to affected communities or the public** |
| **MANAGE 3.2 (Disclosure policies)** | **❌** | **No policies for data/model disclosure to external stakeholders** |

---

## Summary of Gaps

| # | Gap | NIST AI RMF Reference | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No AI system inventory/registry** | GOV 1.6 | Medium |
| 2 | **No decommissioning procedure** (mentioned in scope but not detailed) | GOV 1.7 | Medium |
| 3 | **No model-level explainability** (SHAP, LIME, feature importance, model cards) | §3.5, MEASURE 2.8 | High |
| 4 | **No comprehensive security evaluation** (penetration testing, red-teaming, model extraction defense) | §3.3, MEASURE 2.7 | High |
| 5 | **No privacy-by-design controls** in this SOP (deferred to SOP-DATA-001) | §3.6, MEASURE 2.13 | Medium |
| 6 | **No end-user feedback or appeal mechanism** | MEASURE 3.3 | High |
| 7 | **No consideration of non-AI alternatives** | MANAGE 2.1 | Medium |
| 8 | **No communication of risks to affected communities or the public** | MANAGE 3.1, 3.2 | Medium |
| 9 | **Limited workforce diversity requirements** (only Medical Advisory Board diversity specified) | GOV 3.1 | Low |
| 10 | **No systematic residual risk documentation** for all models (only for exceptions) | MANAGE 1.4 | Low |

---

## Strengths

SOP-CLIN-005 is **exceptionally strong** in the areas that fall within its core focus:

- **Validity & Reliability**: Rigorous pre-clinical and independent clinical validation with strict data separation, bootstrapped CIs, and calibration gating.
- **Safety**: Multi-layered safeguards (pre-clinical hold, confidence thresholding, silent mode, human control, human factors evaluation).
- **Fairness/Bias**: Mandatory subgroup analysis with statistical and clinical significance thresholds, root-cause analysis, and mitigation plans.
- **Accountability**: Clear RACI matrix, separation of developer and validator roles, signed release authorization, immutable audit logs.
- **Measurement**: Comprehensive metrics, independent review, ongoing monitoring at multiple cadences (15-min, monthly, quarterly).

---

## Conclusion

SOP-CLIN-005 satisfies the majority of NIST AI RMF's foundational expectations, particularly in the **MEASURE** function and the trustworthiness characteristics of **validity/reliability, safety, fairness, and accountability**. However, it has meaningful gaps in **model-level explainability, security evaluation depth, end-user appeal mechanisms, privacy integration, decommissioning, AI system inventorying, and communication to external stakeholders**. Several of these gaps (privacy, security, decommissioning) may be addressed by companion SOPs referenced in §11.1, but they are not integrated into this SOP's risk management process as NIST expects under GOV 1.2.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) compliant with HIPAA's expectations for handling PHI referenced in regulatory filings?
question.expected_answer: Gap. SOP-LEGC-005 includes regulatory filings that may reference PHI but lacks foundational HIPAA controls: (1) no PHI de-identification methodology — neither Safe Harbor (§164.514(b)(2)) nor Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)) — for data appearing in filings; (2) breach-notification procedures are entirely missing — no reference to §164.400-414 timelines or required content; (3) no documented minimum-necessary policy (§164.502(b)) for the PHI that filings personnel may access. These are core HIPAA controls; their absence in a regulatory-filings SOP that touches PHI is a material gap.

---

## Compliance Assessment: SOP-LEGC-005 vs. HIPAA Requirements for PHI in Regulatory Filings

### Overview

SOP-LEGC-005 governs the end-to-end lifecycle of regulatory filings at Meridian Health Technologies, a company whose scope explicitly includes HIPAA-covered operations (Clinical AI Platform, HealthPay Financial Services, MedInsight Analytics). The SOP is well-structured for general regulatory filing governance but has **significant gaps** when evaluated specifically against HIPAA's Privacy Rule and Security Rule requirements for handling Protected Health Information (PHI) referenced in or attached to regulatory filings.

---

### ✅ Areas of Compliance (Strengths)

#### 1. Transmission Security — §164.312(e)
The SOP explicitly prohibits submission via "standard unencrypted email without prior approval from the CISO and GC" (Section 5.4, ¶1) and mandates use of designated regulatory portals (FDA eSubmitter, EUDAMED, state AG portals). This directly supports HIPAA's requirement to "implement technical security measures to guard against unauthorized access to electronic protected health information that is being transmitted over an electronic communications network" (§164.312(e)(1)).

#### 2. Access Control & Workforce Security — §164.312(a), §164.308(a)(3)–(4)
- **C-02** enforces role-based access control (RBAC) in ServiceNow, preventing unauthorized modification of the G4 approval gate.
- **C-03** establishes a zero-deletion workspace in iManage with break-glass procedures for post-submission edits.
- **C-06** enforces distinct user IDs for content creators vs. reviewers (4-eyes review).
- Training access revocation after 60 days overdue (Section 9) supports workforce clearance procedures under §164.308(a)(3)(ii)(B).

#### 3. Audit Controls — §164.312(b)
Screen recording of submissions (Section 5.4, ¶3), iManage metadata tagging (Section 5.5, ¶1), and monthly "immutable zone" access log reviews (C-03) collectively satisfy the requirement for "hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use electronic protected health information."

#### 4. Integrity Controls — §164.312(c)
The "Single Version of Truth" policy (Section 4, Policy 2), digitally signed zip archives for evidence packages (Section 5.2, ¶3), and the zero-deletion repository support HIPAA's integrity standard requiring protection of ePHI from improper alteration.

#### 5. Administrative Safeguards — §164.308(a)(1)
The G1–G5 gated review workflow, the RACI matrix with clear accountability, and C-level approval for material filings (Section 5.3) provide a robust administrative control framework consistent with the security management process standard.

#### 6. Breach Incident Routing
The Security Incident Response Lead is designated as RFO for security-incident-related regulatory notifications (Section 3.1), and the AIMS system (C-07) automatically classifies serious incidents. This partially supports the breach notification framework under §164.404 and §164.408.

---

### ❌ Gaps and Deficiencies

#### GAP 1: No Minimum Necessary Standard Applied to PHI in Filings — §164.502(b), §164.514(d)
**This is the most significant gap.** HIPAA's minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to "make reasonable efforts to limit protected health information to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose of the use, disclosure, or request" (§164.502(b)(1)). For routine/recurring disclosures, §164.514(d)(3)(i) requires "policies and procedures (which may be standard protocols) that limit the protected health information disclosed to the amount reasonably necessary." For non-routine disclosures, §164.514(d)(3)(ii) requires criteria and individual review.

The SOP contains **no**:
- Protocol for determining the minimum amount of PHI needed for each filing type
- Review step to verify only minimum-necessary PHI is included
- Criteria for limiting PHI in routine vs. non-routine filings
- Any reference to the minimum necessary standard

> **Note:** While §164.502(b)(2)(v) exempts disclosures "required by law" from minimum necessary, not all regulatory filings are legally mandated—some are permitted but voluntary (e.g., certain public health reports, voluntary FDA MedWatch submissions). The SOP's broad scope means this exemption does not cover all filing scenarios.

#### GAP 2: No De-identification Evaluation — §164.514(a)–(b)
Before including PHI in a regulatory filing, HIPAA expects covered entities to consider whether de-identified data (per §164.514(b)—either Expert Determination or Safe Harbor method) or a limited data set (§164.514(e)) would suffice for the regulatory purpose. The SOP has **no step** in the drafting or review workflow (G1–G5) that evaluates whether de-identified or limited data could be used instead of full PHI. This is a missed opportunity to reduce disclosure risk.

#### GAP 3: No PHI-Specific Classification or Tagging of Filings
The SOP's iManage metadata schema (Section 5.5) includes `Obligation ID`, `Regulator`, `Report_Type`, `Date_of_Submission`, and `Legal_Hold_Flag`—but **no field indicating whether the filing contains PHI**. Without this classification:
- Enhanced security controls cannot be selectively applied to PHI-containing filings
- Audit and accountability for PHI disclosures are weakened
- It is difficult to demonstrate compliance with the minimum necessary standard after the fact

#### GAP 4: No Documentation of HIPAA Legal Authority for PHI Disclosures — §164.512
When PHI is disclosed in a regulatory filing, the specific HIPAA-permitted purpose should be documented (e.g., §164.512(a) "required by law," §164.512(b) "public health activities," §164.512(d) "health oversight activities," §164.512(e) "judicial and administrative proceedings"). The SOP's Evidence Package (Section 5.2, ¶3) and metadata schema do **not require recording which HIPAA exception authorizes the PHI disclosure**. This creates compliance risk if a disclosure is later challenged.

#### GAP 5: No Encryption Specification for Filed Documents Containing ePHI — §164.312(e)(2)(ii), §164.312(a)(2)(iv)
While the SOP prohibits unencrypted email transmission, it does **not specify encryption requirements for the filed documents themselves** (the PDF/A document and the digitally signed zip archive). If these contain ePHI and are stored in iManage or transmitted through portals, the encryption standard (§164.312(a)(2)(iv) and §164.312(e)(2)(ii)) should be explicitly addressed. The SOP references `SOP-SECP-009` (Data Classification and Handling Standard) but does not cross-reference encryption requirements for PHI-containing filing packages.

#### GAP 6: No HIPAA-Specific Training for Filing Personnel — §164.308(a)(5)
The training modules (Section 9) cover regulatory filing essentials (MGT-110), SR 11-7 governance (MGT-210), and duty of candor (MGT-310), but **none include HIPAA Privacy Rule training**—specifically the minimum necessary standard, permitted disclosures under §164.512, or de-identification requirements. Personnel who draft and review filings containing PHI must understand these obligations per §164.308(a)(5) (security awareness and training).

#### GAP 7: No PHI-Specific Breach Scenario for the Filing Process — §164.404, §164.408
The SOP addresses security incident response generally but does **not specifically address breach scenarios unique to the filing process**, such as:
- PHI submitted to the wrong regulatory portal or wrong recipient
- PHI inadvertently included in a filing that should have contained only de-identified data
- Unauthorized access to PHI in the iManage repository by personnel without a need-to-know

HIPAA requires individual notification within 60 days of discovery (§164.404(b)) and Secretary notification (§164.408). The SOP should cross-reference these specific timelines and requirements for filing-related breaches.

---

### Summary Table

| HIPAA Requirement | SOP Coverage | Status |
|---|---|---|
| §164.312(e) Transmission security | Prohibition on unencrypted email; designated portals | ✅ Compliant |
| §164.312(a) Access control | RBAC, zero-deletion, distinct user IDs | ✅ Compliant |
| §164.312(b) Audit controls | Screen recording, metadata, log reviews | ✅ Compliant |
| §164.312(c) Integrity | Single version of truth, digital signatures | ✅ Compliant |
| §164.308(a)(1) Security management | G1–G5 workflow, RACI, C-level approval | ✅ Compliant |
| §164.502(b) / §164.514(d) Minimum necessary | **Not addressed** | ❌ Gap |
| §164.514(a)–(b) De-identification evaluation | **Not addressed** | ❌ Gap |
| PHI classification/tagging in filings | **Not addressed** | ❌ Gap |
| §164.512 Legal authority documentation | **Not addressed** | ❌ Gap |
| §164.312(a)(2)(iv)/(e)(2)(ii) Encryption of filing packages | **Not specified** | ❌ Gap |
| §164.308(a)(5) HIPAA-specific training | **Not included** | ❌ Gap |
| §164.404/§164.408 Filing-specific breach procedures | **Not addressed** | ❌ Gap |

---

### Recommended Remediations

1. **Add a minimum necessary review gate** (or augment G1/G2) requiring the RFO to attest that only the minimum PHI necessary for the filing's regulatory purpose is included, with standard protocols for recurring filing types.
2. **Add a de-identification evaluation step** in the drafting phase (Section 5.2) requiring the RFO to document why de-identified or limited data sets are insufficient if PHI is included.
3. **Add a `Contains_PHI` metadata field** to the iManage schema and require it for all filings.
4. **Require documentation of the applicable §164.512 exception** in the Evidence Package for every filing containing PHI.
5. **Specify encryption standards** (e.g., AES-256) for filing packages containing ePHI, cross-referencing SOP-SECP-009.
6. **Add a HIPAA Privacy Rule module** (or augment MGT-110) covering minimum necessary, permitted disclosures, and de-identification for all filing personnel.
7. **Add filing-specific breach scenarios** to Section 6 or a cross-referenced incident response SOP, with explicit references to §164.404 and §164.408 timelines.

---

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-HR-012 (AI Ethics Committee Governance) satisfy NIST AI RMF GOVERN function expectations?
question.expected_answer: Gap. Ironically for an SOP titled 'AI Ethics Committee Governance', the SOP fails GOVERN: (1) the committee structure is described but lacks the accountability, authority, and reporting lines (GOVERN 2) required to actually function as an AI governance body; (2) no AI risk management framework is adopted or referenced (GOVERN 1); (3) AI system inventory does not exist — the committee cannot govern what it does not enumerate (MAP/GOVERN dependency). Without these foundations, the committee is form without function.

---

## SOP-HR-012 vs. NIST AI RMF GOVERN Function — Detailed Compliance Assessment

The NIST AI RMF (AI 100-1) defines the **GOVERN** function as a cross-cutting function that "cultivates and implements a culture of risk management within organizations designing, developing, deploying, evaluating, or acquiring AI systems" (NIST AI 100-1, §5.1, p. 21). It comprises **6 categories and 19 subcategories** (Table 1, pp. 22–23). Below is a systematic evaluation of SOP-HR-012 against each.

---

### GOVERN 1: Policies, processes, procedures, and practices are in place, transparent, and implemented effectively

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 1.1** – Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented | ✅ **Satisfied** | The SOP explicitly references EU AI Act, HIPAA, GDPR, MDR, and SR 11-7. Legal & Compliance Counsel is a permanent voting member (§3). The DPO provides binding guidance on data permissibility (§3). Mandatory annual EU AI Act legal update training (COMP-401, §9). |
| **GOVERN 1.2** – Characteristics of trustworthy AI are integrated into organizational policies | ✅ **Satisfied** | Policy Statements PS-01 through PS-05 embed key trustworthiness characteristics: safety (CMO veto, PS-01), human oversight (PS-02), transparency/explainability (PS-03), fairness/bias management (PS-04), privacy/data minimization (PS-05). These are operationalized through the review and triage procedures (§5). |
| **GOVERN 1.3** – Processes to determine the needed level of risk management activities based on risk tolerance | ✅ **Satisfied** | The triage process (§5.2) categorizes submissions as "Low Materiality / Fast-Track" or "Full Committee Review Required"—a risk-tiered approach. Quantitative risk tolerances are defined: 5% equal-opportunity difference parity threshold (PS-04) and PSI > 0.25 drift trigger (§5.4). |
| **GOVERN 1.4** – Risk management process and outcomes established through transparent mechanisms | ✅ **Satisfied** | The SOP itself is a transparent policy document. Voting outcomes are recorded in the Master AI Registry. Rejection Briefs must be issued within 72 hours (§5.3). Annual Transparency Report to CEO and Board Audit Committee (§7). Immutable audit trail with WORM storage (§6.1). |
| **GOVERN 1.5** – Ongoing monitoring and periodic review planned, with roles and frequency defined | ✅ **Satisfied** | §5.4 mandates continuous monitoring, triggered re-review, and 24-month mandatory revalidation. §7 defines KPIs with explicit reporting cadences (daily automated scans, weekly escalations, monthly reconciliation, bi-weekly fairness reports). Roles are defined in the RACI matrix (§3). |
| **GOVERN 1.6** – Mechanisms to inventory AI systems, resourced per risk priorities | ✅ **Satisfied** | The AEC-Docket register assigns unique Docket IDs (§5.1). The Master AI Registry tracks all authorized systems. The "Coverage" KPI (§7) requires 100% of active high-risk AI systems in the Registry vs. detected systems, with monthly reconciliation to prevent "shadow AI." |
| **GOVERN 1.7** – Processes for decommissioning and phasing out AI systems safely | ❌ **Gap** | The Purpose and Scope (§1) mentions "decommissioning" as part of the lifecycle covered, but **no specific decommissioning procedure exists**. The 24-month auto-suspension (§5.4) removes systems from production if revalidation isn't completed, but this is a suspension, not a planned decommissioning. There is no guidance on data retention, model archival, transition planning, stakeholder notification, or safe wind-down procedures. |

---

### GOVERN 2: Accountability structures are in place

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 2.1** – Roles, responsibilities, and lines of communication documented and clear | ✅ **Satisfied** | §3 provides a detailed RACI matrix with 9 defined roles and explicit responsibility assignments. Lines of communication are established through the submission protocol (§5.1), escalation ladder (§8.2), and reporting cadences (§7). |
| **GOVERN 2.2** – Personnel and partners receive AI risk management training | ✅ **Satisfied** | §9 defines three mandatory training courses: AEC-101 (member onboarding, 4 hours), AIET-201 (annual for submitters), COMP-401 (annual legal update). Compliance is tracked by the CHRO; overdue training >15 days results in loss of voting privilege. |
| **GOVERN 2.3** – Executive leadership takes responsibility for AI risk decisions | ✅ **Satisfied** | The CAIO chairs the Committee with final decision authority (§3). The CMO holds veto power for patient safety (§5.3). The CEO is the ultimate escalation point (§8.2) and can issue a "Superseding Executive Order" (with mandatory regulatory disclosure). The Annual Transparency Report goes to the CEO and Board Audit Committee (§7). |

---

### GOVERN 3: Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility are prioritized

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 3.1** – Decision-making informed by a diverse team (demographics, disciplines, experience, backgrounds) | ⚠️ **Partial** | The Committee includes cross-functional *disciplinary* diversity: medical (CMO), legal, privacy (DPO), security (CISO), engineering (VPs), HR (CHRO), and external ethics advisors. However, the SOP **does not address demographic diversity, DEIA commitments, or accessibility** in Committee composition or recruitment. NIST specifically calls for "diversity of demographics, disciplines, experience, expertise, and backgrounds"—the SOP only substantively addresses disciplines and expertise. |
| **GOVERN 3.2** – Policies to define and differentiate roles for human-AI configurations and oversight | ⚠️ **Partial** | PS-02 mandates human oversight for high-risk AI and requires the Committee to validate that oversight prevents automation bias. The RACI matrix differentiates governance-level roles. However, the SOP **does not define operational human-AI configuration roles**—e.g., who monitors the system in daily operation, when a human should override, specific human-in-the-loop vs. human-on-the-loop protocols, or operator proficiency requirements at the point of use. |

---

### GOVERN 4: Organizational teams are committed to a culture that considers and communicates AI risk

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 4.1** – Policies foster critical thinking and safety-first mindset | ✅ **Satisfied** | The CMO's "Clear and Unambiguous Imminent Danger to Life" veto (§5.3), mandatory pre-deployment review (PS-01), zero-tolerance bias policy (PS-04), 72-hour RCA for critical adverse events (§7), COI disclosure requirements (§6.2), and whistleblower protections (§6.2) all demonstrate a safety-first, critical-thinking culture. |
| **GOVERN 4.2** – Teams document risks and potential impacts and communicate broadly | ✅ **Satisfied** | The Conformity Assessment Package (§5.1) requires Model Cards, Dataset Datasheets, and Annex IV technical documentation. Triage Reports and Rejection Briefs document risk assessments. The Annual Transparency Report communicates impacts to the Board and forms part of public-facing transparency disclosures (§7). |
| **GOVERN 4.3** – Practices enable AI testing, identification of incidents, and information sharing | ⚠️ **Partial** | Testing and incident identification are well-covered: Fairness-360 dashboard (§5.2), PSI drift monitoring (§5.4), Critical Adverse Event logging with 72-hour RCA (§7). However, **systematic information-sharing practices are not explicitly defined**—there is no cross-team incident database, no lessons-learned dissemination protocol, and no internal communication plan for sharing incident learnings beyond the Committee's own reporting cadence. The ombuds channel (§6.2) is for ethical violations, not general incident information sharing. |

---

### GOVERN 5: Processes for robust engagement with relevant AI actors

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 5.1** – Collect, consider, prioritize, and integrate feedback from those external to the team regarding individual and societal impacts | ❌ **Gap** | The Independent Ethics Advisors provide external, impartial review (§3), and the ombuds channel allows internal reporting (§6.2). However, there is **no structured mechanism for collecting feedback from affected individuals, patients, consumers, or civil society organizations** about the real-world impacts of deployed AI systems. The SOP is almost entirely internally focused. NIST expects engagement with stakeholders "external to the team that developed or deployed the AI system," which includes affected communities, not just appointed advisors. |
| **GOVERN 5.2** – Mechanisms to regularly incorporate adjudicated feedback from relevant AI actors into system design | ⚠️ **Partial** | Re-review triggers (PSI drift, fairness metric drops, adverse events in §5.4) can lead to system modifications. "Conditionally Approved" status with 90-day review contingencies allows for iterative feedback. However, there is **no formal mechanism for systematically incorporating external stakeholder feedback** into design iterations. The feedback loop is primarily internal (Committee → Engineering team). |

---

### GOVERN 6: Policies and procedures to address AI risks from third-party software, data, and supply chain

| Subcategory | Assessment | SOP Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 6.1** – Policies address AI risks associated with third-party entities, including IP risks | ⚠️ **Partial** | The Scope (§1) explicitly includes "Third-party AI components integrated into Meridian products, including APIs and embedded models," which must go through the same Committee review. However, the SOP **does not specifically address IP infringement risks, supply chain due diligence, vendor assessment criteria, contractual IP warranties, or data provenance requirements** for third-party components beyond the general Conformity Assessment package. |
| **GOVERN 6.2** – Contingency processes for failures or incidents in third-party data or AI systems deemed high-risk | ❌ **Gap** | The Emergency Use Authorization (§8.1) is a contingency for internal clinical emergencies, not third-party failures. The auto-suspension at 24 months could theoretically apply to third-party systems, but there is **no documented contingency plan for third-party AI system failures**—e.g., vendor discontinuation, API deprecation, third-party model drift, or supply chain disruption. The CMO's emergency halt and the CAIO's manual toggle could be invoked reactively, but no proactive third-party contingency procedure exists. |

---

## Summary Scorecard

| Category | Subcategories | Satisfied | Partial | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GOVERN 1 (Policies & processes) | 7 | 6 | 0 | 1 (GV-1.7: Decommissioning) |
| GOVERN 2 (Accountability) | 3 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| GOVERN 3 (DEIA) | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 |
| GOVERN 4 (Risk culture) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| GOVERN 5 (Stakeholder engagement) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 (GV-5.1: External feedback) |
| GOVERN 6 (Third-party/supply chain) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 (GV-6.2: Third-party contingency) |
| **Total** | **19** | **11 (58%)** | **5 (26%)** | **3 (16%)** |

---

## Key Gaps Requiring Remediation

1. **GOVERN 1.7 – Decommissioning procedures:** Add a dedicated section defining safe decommissioning workflows: data retention/archival policies, stakeholder notification requirements, transition planning for dependent systems, and criteria for phasing out AI systems without increasing risk.

2. **GOVERN 5.1 – External stakeholder feedback:** Establish structured mechanisms (e.g., patient advisory panels, public comment periods, community impact surveys) to collect, consider, and integrate feedback from individuals and communities affected by deployed AI systems.

3. **GOVERN 6.2 – Third-party contingency planning:** Develop documented contingency procedures for third-party AI system failures, including vendor exit strategies, API deprecation response plans, alternative model sourcing, and contractual requirements for vendor notification of material changes.

## Partial Gaps Worthy of Enhancement

4. **GOVERN 3.1 – Demographic diversity:** Add explicit DEIA requirements for Committee composition and for the selection of Independent Ethics Advisors, aligning with NIST's emphasis on "diversity of demographics."

5. **GOVERN 3.2 – Operational human-AI configuration roles:** Supplement the governance-level oversight with operational procedures defining human-in-the-loop vs. human-on-the-loop protocols, operator proficiency requirements, and override authority at the point of use.

6. **GOVERN 4.3 – Information sharing:** Establish a formal internal incident-sharing protocol (e.g., cross-team incident database, lessons-learned bulletins) beyond Committee-level reporting.

7. **GOVERN 5.2 – External feedback integration:** Create a formal process for adjudicating and incorporating external stakeholder feedback into system design iterations.

8. **GOVERN 6.1 – Third-party IP and supply chain due diligence:** Add specific vendor assessment criteria, IP warranty requirements, and data provenance standards for third-party AI components.

---

Compliance level: partial

question.question: What recent (within the past 12 months) OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency statement addresses banks' use of AI/ML for lending decisions?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite at least one specific recent OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency Statement on AI/ML in lending found via web search, with title, date, and key directive. Naive RAG cannot answer this with currency because the knowledge base contains the underlying guidance (SR 11-7 / SR 26-2) but not subsequent supervisory communications.

expected_citations.section: Recent OCC bulletins / FDIC FILs / interagency statements

output.answer:
Based on my research, here is a comprehensive summary of the most relevant recent regulatory actions addressing banks' use of AI/ML for lending decisions:

---

## Key Finding: No Dedicated Interagency Statement on AI/ML Lending

There is **no single, dedicated OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency statement issued in the past 12 months that exclusively addresses AI/ML in lending decisions**. However, several closely related issuances directly bear on the topic:

---

### 1. OCC Bulletin 2025-26 — "Model Risk Management: Clarification for Community Banks" (September 18, 2025)

This is the **most directly relevant OCC issuance**. While it does not use the terms "artificial intelligence" or "AI," it clarifies model risk management (MRM) expectations for community banks (assets up to $30 billion), and AI/ML models used in lending fall squarely within the MRM framework (SR 11-7 / OCC Bulletin 2011-12).

**Key points:**
- Community banks should **tailor MRM practices** commensurate with their risk exposures, business activities, and the complexity and extent of model use — not apply large-bank standards by default.
- The bulletin **does not require annual model validation** for community banks, providing flexibility for AI/ML model oversight.
- This signals the OCC's intent to make it easier for community banks to deploy AI while maintaining appropriate risk controls.

**Source:** [OCC Bulletin 2025-26](https://www.occ.gov); [Forvis Mazars analysis](https://www.forvismazars.us/forsights/2025/10/occ-issues-bulletins-to-reduce-regulatory-burden-for-community-banks)

---

### 2. CFPB Supervisory Highlights — Special Edition on Advanced Technologies (January 17, 2025)

While not an OCC/FDIC issuance, this is the **most targeted recent supervisory publication on AI/ML in credit decisions**:

- Examined institutions using **credit scoring models built with AI/ML technology** for underwriting and pricing.
- Highlighted **fair lending risks**, including disparate impact and lack of explainability.
- Emphasized the need for **documentation of model selection processes** and **testing for discriminatory outcomes**.
- Lenders must ensure AI models do not use **prohibited factors** under ECOA and the Fair Housing Act.

**Source:** [CFPB Supervisory Highlights, Winter 2025](https://www.consumerfinance.gov); [Compliance Cohort summary](https://www.compliancecohort.com/blog/cfpb-releases-winter-2025-edition-of-supervisory-highlights)

---

### 3. U.S. Treasury Report — "The Uses, Opportunities, and Risks of Artificial Intelligence in Financial Services" (December 19, 2024)

Issued following Treasury's June 2024 RFI, this report:

- Highlights **increasing AI use throughout the financial sector**, including credit underwriting.
- Recommends **aligning definitions of AI models and systems** applicable to the financial sector.
- Calls for **enhanced risk management frameworks** and **improved information sharing** on AI risks (bias, data privacy, security).
- Recommends that regulators and financial institutions address **fair lending and consumer protection risks** specific to AI/ML.

**Source:** [U.S. Treasury Press Release](https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy2760); [Debevoise analysis](https://www.debevoise.com/-/media/files/insights/publications/2025/01/treasurys-post-2024-rfi-report-on-ai-in-financial.pdf)

---

### 4. OCC Speeches & RFIs on AI (2024–2025)

| Date | ID | Description |
|------|----|-------------|
| April 29, 2025 | NR 2025-38 | Acting Comptroller Hood discussed AI in financial services at the National Fair Housing Alliance's Responsible AI Symposium, emphasizing **ethical use, financial inclusion, and innovation**. |
| October 7, 2024 | NR 2024-115 | OCC solicited research on AI in banking and finance. |
| June 6, 2024 | NR 2024-61 | Acting Comptroller discussed AI and **financial stability**. |

**Source:** [OCC News Release 2025-38](https://www.occ.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2025/nr-occ-2025-38.html)

---

### 5. FDIC 2024 Risk Review

The FDIC's annual risk review discusses AI/ML in the context of:

- **Credit decisioning** — AI can improve efficiency but introduces **bias, explainability, and security risks**.
- **Fair lending** — Banks must manage AI explainability and bias to ensure compliance.
- **Third-party risk** — Emphasis on vendor oversight for AI/ML tools.

**Source:** [FDIC 2024 Risk Review](https://www.fdic.gov)

---

### 6. FHFA Advisory Bulletin — AI/ML Risk Management (2024)

While FHFA oversees Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac (not OCC/FDIC-supervised banks), this advisory bulletin is notable for:

- Addressing **model drift** in dynamic ML systems that update continuously.
- Requiring **bias detection, transparency, and compliance** in AI/ML used for mortgage credit decisions.
- Setting expectations for **Enterprise-level AI/ML risk management frameworks**.

**Source:** [Harris Beach Murtha analysis](https://www.harrisbeachmurtha.com/insights/fannie-mae-and-freddie-mac-set-new-ai-standards-for-mortgage-lenders)

---

### 7. Updated Interagency Model Risk Management Guidance (April 17, 2026)

The OCC, Federal Reserve, and FDIC issued **updated interagency MRM guidance**, formally rescinding SR 11-7 and related agency issuances. This is the most recent interagency action directly relevant to AI/ML model governance in lending, though it post-dates the 12-month window for some users.

**Source:** [Bonadio Group analysis](https://www.bonadio.com/article/occs-updated-model-risk-management-guidance)

---

## Summary Table

| Issuance | Date | Agency | AI/ML Lending Focus |
|----------|------|--------|-------------------|
| OCC Bulletin 2025-26 | Sep 18, 2025 | OCC | Indirect — MRM clarification encompassing AI/ML models |
| CFPB Supervisory Highlights (Special Ed.) | Jan 17, 2025 | CFPB | **Direct** — AI/ML credit scoring & fair lending |
| Treasury AI Report | Dec 19, 2024 | Treasury | Broad — AI in financial services including lending |
| FDIC 2024 Risk Review | 2024 | FDIC | Broad — AI risks in credit decisioning |
| OCC NR 2025-38 (Speech) | Apr 29, 2025 | OCC | Broad — responsible AI in financial services |
| FHFA AB on AI/ML | 2024 | FHFA | Direct — AI/ML risk management for mortgage lending |
| Interagency MRM Update | Apr 17, 2026 | OCC/FRB/FDIC | Direct — replaces SR 11-7, governs AI/ML model risk |

---

## Key Takeaway

**No OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency statement in the past 12 months is specifically titled or dedicated to AI/ML in lending decisions.** The regulatory approach has been to address AI/ML through the **existing model risk management framework** (SR 11-7 / OCC Bulletin 2011-12, now updated in 2026) and through **fair lending supervisory scrutiny** (CFPB Supervisory Highlights). The OCC's Bulletin 2025-26 is the closest prudential regulator action, as it clarifies how community banks should right-size MRM — which is the framework that governs AI/ML lending models. Banks should expect examiners to apply MRM and fair lending expectations to AI/ML credit models even in the absence of AI-specific guidance.

question.question: What's the current implementation status of NYC Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools) and Colorado SB 24-205 (Colorado AI Act)? Cite the most recent regulator guidance or enforcement.
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite the latest implementation status (NYC DCWP rules / audit requirements / enforcement updates; Colorado AG rule-making, effective dates, and any safe-harbor guidance). Naive RAG cannot answer — these are US state/municipal laws not in the EU regulation knowledge base.

output.answer:
# Implementation Status: NYC Local Law 144 & Colorado SB 24-205

---

## NYC Local Law 144 — Automated Employment Decision Tools (AEDTs)

### Current Status: **In Effect & Undergoing Enforcement Overhaul**

Local Law 144 has been **fully effective and enforceable since July 5, 2023**. The NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP) is the designated enforcement agency, with authority to impose civil penalties of **$500–$1,500 per violation per day**.

### Key Requirements (Operational Now)
- **Annual bias audits** of AEDTs by an independent auditor, with results publicly posted.
- **Advance notice** to candidates/residents at least 10 business days before an AEDT is used.
- **Alternative selection processes** or reasonable accommodations upon request.

### Most Recent Regulatory Development: NY State Comptroller Audit (December 2025)

The most significant recent development is the **New York State Comptroller's audit released December 2, 2025**, which evaluated DCWP's enforcement of LL 144 and concluded it is **"ineffective."** Key findings:

| Finding | Detail |
|---|---|
| **311 complaint misrouting** | 75% of test calls about AEDT issues to NYC's 311 hotline were misrouted and never reached DCWP |
| **DCWP undercounted violations** | DCWP surveyed 32 companies and found just 1 case of non-compliance; Comptroller auditors reviewing the same companies identified **at least 17 potential violations** |
| **Inadequate complaint handling** | No standardized intake procedures; incomplete documentation of complaints |
| **Limited proactive enforcement** | DCWP relied almost entirely on complaints rather than proactive investigation |

**Source:** [NY State Comptroller Audit — Enforcement of Local Law 144 (Dec. 2, 2025)](https://www.osc.ny.gov/state-agencies/audits/2025/12/02/enforcement-local-law-144-automated-employment-decision-tools)

### DCWP Response & What It Means for Employers

DCWP has committed to implementing the audit's recommendations, including:
- Fixing 311 routing for AEDT complaints
- Standardizing complaint intake and documentation
- Leveraging the NYC Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI) Enforcement Workbook for bias audit reviews
- Increasing proactive enforcement activity

**Practical implication:** Employers should expect **significantly heightened enforcement** in 2026 as DCWP corrects the deficiencies identified. Each use of a non-compliant AEDT may constitute a separate violation, meaning high-volume employers could accumulate penalties of **$15,000–$45,000 per AEDT per month** of non-compliance.

**Source:** [DLA Piper — Critical Audit of NYC's AI Hiring Law (Jan. 30, 2026)](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/2026/01/critical-audit-of-nyc-ai-hiring-law-signals-increased-risk-for-employers)

---

## Colorado SB 24-205 — Colorado AI Act

### Current Status: **Enactment Complete; Operative Date Delayed to June 30, 2026**

Governor Jared Polis signed SB 24-205 in **May 2024**, making Colorado the first U.S. state to enact a comprehensive AI regulatory framework. However, the law is **not yet in effect**.

### Timeline of Amendments

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| **May 2024** | SB 24-205 signed into law; original effective date set for February 1, 2026 |
| **2025 session** | SB 25-318 introduced to narrow and delay the Act — **failed to advance** (sponsor moved to postpone indefinitely) |
| **August 28, 2025** | Governor Polis signed **SB 25B-004** ("Increase Transparency for Algorithmic Systems Act") following a special legislative session, **delaying all operative dates from February 1, 2026 to June 30, 2026** |

**Source:** [Baker Botts — Colorado AI Act Implementation Delayed (Sept. 2025)](https://www.bakerbotts.com/thought-leadership/publications/2025/september/colorado-ai-act-implementation-delayed); [Institute for Evidence-based Policymaking — AI Policy in Colorado](https://www.evidence-based.org/ai-policy-in-colorado)

### What SB 25B-004 Changed (and Didn't Change)

- **All substantive obligations remain unchanged** — the delay is purely a timing extension.
- The delay provides the Colorado legislature additional time during the **2026 session** to consider further amendments before the law takes effect.
- Governor Polis has consistently expressed reservations about the "complex compliance regime" and urged simplification.

### Key Requirements (Effective June 30, 2026, Unless Further Amended)

**For Developers:**
- Protect consumers from "reasonably foreseeable risks" of algorithmic discrimination
- Provide disclosures and documentation to deployers and the public

**For Deployers:**
- Implement risk management policies and practices for "high-risk" AI systems
- Conduct impact assessments for AI systems that make or substantially influence **"consequential decisions"** (including employment, insurance, education, housing, and more)
- Provide consumers notice when AI is used in consequential decisions
- Allow consumers to opt out and request human review

### No Formal Rulemaking or Enforcement Guidance Yet

As of the latest available information, **Colorado has not yet issued formal implementing regulations, rulemaking notices, or enforcement guidance** under SB 24-205. The Attorney General's office will have exclusive enforcement authority once the law is operative. There is no private right of action.

**Source:** [DLA Piper GENIE — Colorado: Groundbreaking AI Law Faces Pushback (2025)](https://knowledge.dlapiper.com/dlapiperknowledge/globalemploymentlatestdevelopments/2025/Colorados-groundbreaking-AI-law-Progress-pushback-and-timeline-shift)

---

## Side-by-Side Comparison

| Dimension | NYC LL 144 | Colorado SB 24-205 |
|---|---|---|
| **Status** | In effect since July 2023 | Enacted but not yet operative |
| **Operative Date** | July 5, 2023 | June 30, 2026 (delayed from Feb. 1, 2026) |
| **Scope** | Hiring & promotion AEDTs only | All "high-risk" AI systems making "consequential decisions" |
| **Enforcement Agency** | NYC DCWP | Colorado Attorney General (exclusive) |
| **Current Enforcement** | Active but found "ineffective" per Dec. 2025 audit; overhaul underway | None yet — law not yet operative |
| **Penalties** | $500–$1,500/violation/day | Colorado Consumer Protection Act penalties; AG enforcement |
| **Most Recent Development** | NY State Comptroller audit (Dec. 2025) demanding enforcement improvements | SB 25B-004 delay (Aug. 2025); further amendments possible in 2026 session |
| **Private Right of Action** | No | No |

---

## Key Takeaways

1. **NYC LL 144 is live and enforceable now.** The December 2025 Comptroller audit signals that enforcement will intensify — employers should ensure bias audits are current, notices are posted, and alternative processes are available.

2. **Colorado SB 24-205 is not yet in effect.** The June 30, 2026 operative date gives organizations time to prepare, but the law's fate remains uncertain — further amendments or even additional delays are possible during the 2026 legislative session.

3. **Both laws lack private rights of action**, but regulatory enforcement (DCWP in NYC; AG in Colorado) can still result in significant penalties.

question.question: What's the most recent UK ICO guidance on AI and data protection, and how does it differ from EDPB guidance under GDPR?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite the most recent ICO AI guidance (e.g. ICO + Alan Turing Institute joint guidance, ICO AI auditing framework, recent ICO calls for views) by title and date, plus note divergences from EDPB positions (e.g. on legitimate interest as a basis for training, on facial recognition, on automated decisions). Naive cannot answer — these are post-knowledge-base regulator outputs.

output.answer:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Here's the detailed comparison:

---

## Most Recent UK ICO Guidance on AI and Data Protection

The ICO's primary guidance is the **"Guidance on AI and Data Protection"** (most recently updated in 2023, restructured around UK GDPR data protection principles), supplemented by its **AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit** (2022) and its **Consultation Response on Generative AI and Data Protection** (published 10 December 2024).

**Key features of the ICO's current position:**

1. **Structured around UK GDPR principles** — The guidance is organised by lawfulness, fairness, transparency, and accountability, rather than by AI lifecycle stage. ([ICO Guidance on AI and Data Protection](https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/artificial-intelligence/guidance-on-ai-and-data-protection))

2. **Legitimate interests as the preferred lawful basis for AI training** — The ICO's December 2024 consultation response endorses legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f) UK GDPR) as a viable legal basis for web-scraping and training generative AI models "in some circumstances," provided the controller completes a rigorous three-part balancing test (purpose, necessity, balancing). The ICO emphasises that this is not a blanket permission and that heightened scrutiny applies. ([Paul Weiss memo, Dec 2024](https://www.paulweiss.com/insights/client-memos/european-regulators-provide-guidance-on-the-use-of-personal-data-in-artificial-intelligence))

3. **"Recognised legitimate interests" under the Data (Use and Access) Act (DUAA) 2025** — The DUAA, which received Royal Assent on 19 June 2025, introduces a new concept of "recognised legitimate interests" into the UK GDPR (Schedule 4, Annex 1 conditions). This creates a **closed list of purposes** where the balancing test is deemed satisfied by statute — a significant divergence from the EU GDPR. The ICO is currently updating its guidance to reflect this change. ([Bratby Law analysis](https://bratby.law/recognised-legitimate-interests-duaa-uk-eu-divergence))

4. **Risk-based, context-dependent approach** — The ICO's AI Risk Toolkit emphasises that risks are "heavily context-dependent" and vary across AI applications. DPIAs are expected for high-risk AI processing. ([DLA Piper, 2022](https://privacymatters.dlapiper.com/2022/06/uk-ico-publishes-ai-and-data-protection-risk-toolkit))

5. **Pro-innovation framing** — The ICO's guidance sits within the UK government's cross-sectoral AI governance framework (White Paper on a Pro-Innovation Approach to AI Regulation), built around five principles: safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, and contestability. ([Bratby Law](https://bratby.law/uk-judiciary-ico-ai-use-guidelines))

6. **Under review** — The ICO has flagged that its AI guidance is under review due to the DUAA 2025 coming into effect.

---

## EDPB Guidance Under GDPR (Opinion 28/2024, adopted 17 December 2024)

The EDPB's **Opinion 28/2024** on "certain data protection aspects related to the processing of personal data in the context of AI models" is the most recent EU-level guidance. It was requested by the Irish Data Protection Commission and addresses four specific questions:

1. **When is an AI model considered "anonymous"?** — The EDPB concludes that AI models trained on personal data **cannot in all cases be considered anonymous**. Anonymity must be assessed on a **case-by-case basis**, considering whether personal data can be extracted (directly or through queries) with negligible probability for each data subject. The risk of **memorisation and regurgitation** means many models will not qualify as anonymous. ([EDPB Opinion 28/2024, para. 34](https://www.edpb.europa.eu/system/files/2024-12/edpb_opinion_202428_ai-models_en.pdf))

2. **Legitimate interests in the development phase** — The EDPB confirms legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f) EU GDPR) can be an appropriate legal basis for AI model development, but requires a **context-specific, rigorous balancing test** with heightened scrutiny. The EDPB does **not** endorse it as the default or preferred basis — other lawful bases (consent, contract, etc.) remain equally valid depending on context. ([Securiti AI summary](https://securiti.ai/summary-of-edpb-opinion-282024-concerning-ai-models-processing-of-personal-data))

3. **Legitimate interests in the deployment phase** — Similar analysis applies, but the EDPB emphasises that the balancing test must be conducted separately for deployment, as the purpose, nature of the data, and impact on data subjects may differ significantly from development.

4. **Consequences of unlawful processing in development** — If personal data was unlawfully processed during development, supervisory authorities may impose fines, temporary processing limitations, or erasure orders. However, the EDPB takes a **proportionate approach**: if the model's subsequent operation does not itself process personal data, the unlawfulness of the development phase does not automatically render the model's operation unlawful. ([William Fry analysis](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/unwrapping-the-edpbs-guidance-on-ai-models-and-data-protection))

The EDPB has also established a **Generative AI Enforcement Task Force** (renamed from the ChatGPT Task Force in 2025) to coordinate enforcement actions across EU DPAs.

---

## Key Differences Between ICO and EDPB Guidance

| Dimension | UK ICO | EDPB (Opinion 28/2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal framework** | UK GDPR + DUAA 2025 (diverging from EU GDPR) | EU GDPR (harmonised across 27 Member States) |
| **Legitimate interests** | Favours legitimate interests as the primary basis for AI training; DUAA 2025 introduces "recognised legitimate interests" where the balancing test is **statutorily deemed satisfied** for certain purposes | Accepts legitimate interests as one of several possible bases; does **not** treat it as preferred; insists on full, context-specific balancing test every time |
| **Anonymisation of AI models** | No detailed position yet on when an AI model is "anonymous"; guidance under review | Detailed criteria: model is anonymous only if probability of extracting personal data is **negligible for each data subject**; memorisation/regurgitation risks must be assessed case-by-case |
| **Consequences of unlawful development** | Not directly addressed in current guidance | Explicitly addressed: unlawful development does not automatically taint deployment, but supervisory authorities may impose fines, erasure, or processing limitations |
| **Regulatory philosophy** | Pro-innovation, risk-based, sector-led; aligned with UK White Paper principles (safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, contestability) | Rights-protective, harmonised; emphasises that "innovation must not come at the expense of privacy and accountability" |
| **Enforcement coordination** | Single national regulator (ICO) | Coordinated via EDPB + Generative AI Enforcement Task Force across 27 DPAs |
| **Web scraping for AI training** | ICO acknowledges legitimate interests can apply "in some circumstances" but flags heightened scrutiny for web scraping | EDPB applies the same balancing test framework but does not give web scraping special treatment; all processing must meet the same standard |
| **Statutory safe harbours** | DUAA 2025 creates "recognised legitimate interests" — a closed list where balancing is pre-satisfied by law | No equivalent concept under EU GDPR; every legitimate interest claim requires individual assessment |
| **Guidance structure** | Organised by UK GDPR principles (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, accountability) | Organised by specific legal questions (anonymity, lawful basis in development, lawful basis in deployment, consequences of unlawfulness) |
| **Status** | Under review due to DUAA 2025 | Non-binding opinion, but influential across EU DPAs |

---

## Summary of the Divergence

The most significant structural divergence is the **UK's introduction of "recognised legitimate interests"** under the DUAA 2025, which creates a statutory shortcut for the balancing test — something the EU GDPR does not have and the EDPB would be unlikely to endorse given its emphasis on case-by-case assessment. This means that for certain specified purposes (e.g., national security, public security, certain scientific research), UK controllers can rely on legitimate interests without conducting the full balancing test, while EU controllers must always perform it.

Both regulators are **broadly aligned** on core principles — both accept legitimate interests as a viable basis for AI training, both require DPIAs for high-risk AI, and both emphasise transparency and accountability. However, the **EDPB is more cautious and detailed** on anonymisation (explicitly addressing memorisation and extraction risks), while the **ICO is more permissive and innovation-friendly**, particularly post-DUAA 2025.

Organisations operating across both jurisdictions should treat UK GDPR and EU GDPR as **distinct regimes** and build separate compliance documentation for each, particularly around lawful basis assessments for AI training data.

question.question: What recent SEC enforcement actions or staff guidance address model governance and AI use in trading, advisory, or disclosure?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite at least one SEC enforcement action, examination priorities document, or risk-alert addressing AI in investment advice, model-governance failures, or AI-related disclosure issues, with date and key takeaway. Naive cannot answer — SEC outputs are not in the knowledge base.

expected_citations.section: SEC enforcement / Risk Alerts / Examination Priorities

output.answer:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile the answer.

---

## Recent SEC Enforcement Actions & Staff Guidance on Model Governance and AI Use in Trading, Advisory, and Disclosure

### 1. AI-Washing Enforcement Actions (2024–2025)

The SEC's most visible enforcement activity around AI has been **"AI-washing" cases** — actions against firms that made materially false or misleading claims about their AI capabilities.

| Date | Respondent | Key Facts | Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **March 18, 2024** | **Delphia (USA) Inc.** | Registered investment adviser falsely claimed it used AI/machine learning in its investment process; systemic exaggeration in marketing materials. | $225,000 civil penalty; cease-and-desist |
| **March 18, 2024** | **Global Predictions Inc.** | Registered investment adviser made misleading statements about AI-driven investment strategies, including claiming to be the "first regulated AI financial advisor." | $175,000 civil penalty; cease-and-desist |
| **Jan. 14, 2025** | **Presto Automation Inc.** | First AI-washing action against a **public company**. Presto made materially false claims in SEC filings and public statements about its AI product "Presto Voice" (drive-thru speech recognition), overstating AI autonomy when human intervention was heavily involved. Also charged with failing to file required reports. | Cease-and-desist (consented) |
| **April 9, 2025** | **Albert Saniger (Nate Inc.)** | SEC and DOJ parallel actions; founder/CEO of privately-held startup falsely claimed the company's app used AI to complete purchases, when it largely relied on human workers. | Ongoing litigation |

**Takeaway:** The SEC has made clear that AI-washing is an **immediate enforcement priority**. Senior officials from the Enforcement Division and the new Cyber and Emerging Technologies Unit (CETU) reiterated at the Securities Enforcement Forum West (May 2025) that "rooting out" AI-washing fraud is a top focus. ([DLA Piper, 2025](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/ai-outlook/2025/sec-emphasizes-focus-on-ai-washing))

---

### 2. New Enforcement Unit: Cyber and Emerging Technologies Unit (CETU)

- **February 20, 2025:** The SEC announced the creation of the **Cyber and Emerging Technologies Unit (CETU)**, replacing the prior Crypto Assets and Cyber Unit. CETU is composed of fraud specialists and attorneys from multiple SEC offices and is specifically tasked with combating misconduct related to AI and other emerging technologies, with a focus on protecting retail investors. ([Cooley SLE, Feb. 2025](https://sle.cooley.com/2025/02/25/sec-announces-new-cyber-and-emerging-technologies-unit))

---

### 3. Withdrawal of the Predictive Data Analytics (PDA) Rule Proposal

- **July 2023:** Under Chair Gensler, the SEC proposed the **Predictive Data Analytics Rule** (Release No. 34-97990; File No. S7-12-23), which would have required broker-dealers and investment advisers to:
  - Identify and **eliminate (not just disclose)** conflicts of interest arising from use of "covered technology" (broadly defined to include AI, ML, algorithms, and other predictive models).
  - Maintain written **policies and procedures** for PDA/conflict evaluation.
  - Conduct **testing and monitoring** of covered technologies.

- **June 12, 2025:** The current Commission **formally withdrew** the PDA rule proposal along with 13 other outstanding rule proposals from the prior administration. The withdrawal was published in the Federal Register. ([Federal Register, June 17, 2025](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/06/17/2025-11110/withdrawal-of-proposed-regulatory-actions); [SEC.gov](https://www.sec.gov/rules-regulations/2025/06/s7-12-23))

**Implication:** There is currently **no SEC-specific rule** mandating conflict elimination or model governance for AI/PDA in advisory or trading. However, existing fiduciary, suitability, and supervisory obligations still apply (see §5 below).

---

### 4. SEC Roundtables & Advisory Committee Guidance

#### a. SEC AI Roundtables (March & May 2025)

- **March 27, 2025:** The SEC hosted a roundtable on AI in the financial industry, designed in part to "reset" the agency's approach after the criticized PDA proposal. Topics included risks, benefits, and governance of AI. ([InvestmentLawWatch, Mar. 2025](https://www.investmentlawwatch.com/2025/03/31/secs-approach-to-artificial-intelligence-begins-to-take-shape))

- **May 2025:** A second roundtable focused on:
  - AI-related benefits, costs, and uses
  - Fraud and cybersecurity
  - **Governance and risk management** ([Congress.gov CRS Report](https://www.congress.gov/crs_external_products/IF/HTML/IF13103.html))

#### b. Investor Advisory Committee (IAC) AI Disclosure Recommendation (December 4, 2025)

On December 4, 2025, the SEC's Investor Advisory Committee approved a recommendation that the Commission issue guidance requiring issuers to:

1. **Disclose whether AI has a material impact** on the company;
2. **Describe the nature of AI deployment** (internal operations vs. consumer-facing); and
3. **Report separately**, if material, on how issuers are deploying AI and the effects on (a) internal business operations and (b) consumer-facing matters.

The recommendation was not unanimous — two members opposed it citing increased procedural burdens. The SEC has not yet acted on the recommendation. ([SEC.gov approved recommendation PDF](https://www.sec.gov/files/approved-artificial-intelligence-disclosure-recommendation-120425.pdf); [Crowell, Dec. 2025](https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/investor-advisory-committee-recommends-sec-disclosure-guidelines-for-artificial-intelligence))

---

### 5. Existing Regulatory Obligations That Apply to AI (No New Rules Needed)

Although the PDA rule was withdrawn, regulators have emphasized that **existing rules already govern AI use**:

| Framework | Application to AI |
|---|---|
| **Investment Advisers Act (fiduciary duty)** | Advisers using AI must still act in clients' best interests; AI-driven conflicts must be disclosed and managed. |
| **Securities Exchange Act §15(c)** | Broker-dealers must supervise AI-based trading and recommendation tools under existing supervisory obligations. |
| **FINRA Regulatory Notice 24-09 (June 2024)** | Called for "reasonably designed supervisory systems" for AI tools, with specific focus on **technology governance, reliability, and accuracy of AI models**. Confirmed that existing business continuity requirements apply to AI systems. ([CCH/Wolters Kluwer, Oct. 2025](https://business.cch.com/srd/SP_AI-Regulation-When-New-Tech-Meets-Old-Rules_10-01-2025_final.pdf)) |
| **SEC antifraud provisions (§10(b), Rule 10b-5)** | Materially misleading statements about AI capabilities constitute fraud — the basis for all AI-washing actions. |

---

### 6. SEC AI Task Force (August 2025)

In August 2025, the SEC launched an internal **AI Task Force** charged with "accelerating AI integration to bolster the SEC's mission" — focused on the SEC's own use of AI for surveillance and enforcement (e.g., detecting anomalous trading patterns for insider trading), not on regulating industry AI use. ([Congress.gov CRS Report](https://www.congress.gov/crs_external_products/IF/HTML/IF13103.html))

---

### Summary of Key Takeaways

1. **AI-washing is the SEC's top AI enforcement priority.** Four public actions since March 2024, with CETU officials signaling more to come.
2. **The PDA rule is dead** (withdrawn June 2025), but existing fiduciary, supervisory, and antifraud obligations fully apply to AI/model use in trading and advisory.
3. **FINRA RN 24-09** is the most specific current guidance on AI model governance — requiring reasonably designed supervisory systems, technology governance, and model reliability/accuracy testing.
4. **Disclosure guidance is evolving.** The IAC's December 2025 recommendation pushes for material AI-impact disclosure, but the Commission has not yet adopted it.
5. **No SEC-specific model risk management rule exists** comparable to the OCC's SR 11-7, but the same principles (independent validation, ongoing monitoring, governance) are effectively required through supervisory obligations and fiduciary duty.
