expected_citations.section: Commission implementing acts / AI Office outputs

output.answer:
Based on my research across the regulation knowledge base and current web sources, here is a comprehensive inventory of what the European Commission and AI Office have published in the past 12 months under the EU AI Act.

---

## I. Codes of Practice

### 1. General-Purpose AI (GPAI) Code of Practice
- **Published:** 10 July 2025
- **Approved/confirmed adequate by the Commission:** 1 August 2025
- **Legal basis:** Article 56 of the AI Act (Codes of Practice for GPAI models under Articles 53 and 55)
- **Scope:** Covers three chapters — **Transparency**, **Copyright**, and **Systemic Risk** — providing the first detailed compliance framework for GPAI model providers under the AI Act.
- **Status:** The Commission and the AI Board confirmed the Code is an **adequate voluntary tool** for demonstrating compliance. It was **not** adopted via implementing act (which would have given it "general validity" under Article 56(6)); instead, it remains a voluntary adherence mechanism.
- **Key deadlines:** Codes of practice were required to be ready by 2 May 2025 (Article 56(9)); if inadequate by 2 August 2025, the Commission could have issued implementing acts with common rules. Since the Code was deemed adequate, no fallback implementing acts were triggered.
- **Source:** [European Commission Digital Strategy page](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/contents-code-gpai)

### 2. Code of Practice on Transparency of AI-Generated Content (Draft)
- **First draft published:** 17 December 2025
- **Legal basis:** Article 50 of the AI Act (transparency obligations for AI-generated content, including watermarking and labeling)
- **Scope:** Establishes technical standards for watermarking and detecting synthetic media; targets deployers of AI systems that generate synthetic audio, image, video, or text content.
- **Status:** **Draft** — developed by independent experts commissioned by the AI Office. Final approval expected around June 2026, ahead of Article 50 obligations becoming legally binding on 2 August 2026.
- **Source:** [Cooley insight, 18 Dec 2025](https://www.cooley.com/news/insight/2025/2025-12-18-eu-ai-act-first-draft-code-of-practice-on-transparency-and-watermarking-released); [Jones Day, Jan 2026](https://www.jonesday.com/en/insights/2026/01/european-commission-publishes-draft-code-of-practice-on-ai-labelling-and-transparency)

---

## II. Commission Guidelines (Article 96)

While not legally binding implementing or delegated acts, these guidelines are the Commission's primary interpretive instruments under Article 96(1) and are critical for practical implementation:

### 3. Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices
- **Published:** 4 February 2025
- **Legal basis:** Article 96(1), clarifying Article 5
- **Scope:** Legal explanations and practical examples of the eight categories of prohibited AI practices (manipulative/deceptive AI, exploitative AI, social scoring, predictive policing, etc.). Separate guidelines for additional prohibited practice categories are expected later.
- **Source:** [European Commission Digital Strategy](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/commission-publishes-guidelines-prohibited-artificial-intelligence-ai-practices-defined-ai-act)

### 4. Guidelines on the Definition of an AI System
- **Published:** 4 February 2025
- **Legal basis:** Article 96(1), clarifying Article 3(1)
- **Scope:** Clarifies the machine-based definition of "AI system," including autonomy, adaptability post-deployment, and the generation of predictions, recommendations, or decisions. Helps stakeholders determine whether their software falls within the AI Act's scope.
- **Source:** [Covington analysis](https://www.globalpolicywatch.com/2025/06/european-commission-guidelines-on-the-definition-of-an-ai-system)

### 5. Guidelines on the Scope of Obligations for GPAI Models
- **Published:** 18 July 2025
- **Legal basis:** Article 96(1), clarifying Chapter V (Articles 51–56)
- **Scope:** Interpretive guidance on the definition and scope of GPAI models, lifecycle obligations, systemic risk classification criteria (including the compute threshold of 10^25 FLOPs), and notification duties for providers. Complements the GPAI Code of Practice.
- **Status:** Published in draft; will be formally adopted once translated into all EU languages.
- **Source:** [WilmerHale analysis, 24 Jul 2025](https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/blogs/wilmerhale-privacy-and-cybersecurity-law/20250724-european-commission-issues-guidelines-for-providers-of-general-purpose-ai-models)

---

## III. Implementing Acts

**No formal implementing acts have been adopted to date.** The AI Act grants the Commission implementing-act powers in several areas:

| Power | Legal Basis | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Approve a Code of Practice with general validity | Art. 56(6) | **Not used** — the GPAI Code was confirmed adequate but not elevated to a binding implementing act |
| Common rules for GPAI obligations if Code is inadequate | Art. 56(9) | **Not triggered** — Code was deemed adequate before the 2 Aug 2025 deadline |
| Technical documentation for GPAI models | Art. 53(5) | **Not yet adopted** |
| Detailed arrangements for real-time remote biometric identification | Art. 5(4) | **Not yet adopted** |

---

## IV. Delegated Acts

**No delegated acts have been adopted to date.** The AI Act grants the Commission delegated-act powers for:

| Power | Legal Basis | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Amend Annex I (AI techniques and approaches) | Art. 3(1) | **Not yet exercised** |
| Amend Annex III (high-risk AI use cases) | Art. 7 | **Not yet exercised** |
| Amend Annex VII (technical documentation) | Art. 11 | **Not yet exercised** |
| Amend Annex XI (conformity assessment) | Art. 43(5) | **Not yet exercised** |

---

## V. Summary Timeline

| Date | Instrument | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 4 Feb 2025 | Guidelines | Prohibited AI Practices (Art. 5) |
| 4 Feb 2025 | Guidelines | Definition of an AI System (Art. 3(1)) |
| 10 Jul 2025 | Code of Practice | GPAI Code of Practice (Arts. 53, 55) |
| 18 Jul 2025 | Guidelines | Scope of GPAI Model Obligations (Ch. V) |
| 1 Aug 2025 | Adequacy Confirmation | Commission & AI Board confirm GPAI Code is adequate |
| 17 Dec 2025 | Draft Code of Practice | Transparency of AI-Generated Content (Art. 50) |

---

## Key Takeaways

1. **The GPAI Code of Practice (July 2025) is the most significant deliverable to date** — it is the primary compliance tool for GPAI model providers and was confirmed adequate, avoiding the need for fallback implementing acts.
2. **The Article 50 Transparency Code of Practice (December 2025 draft) is still in development** and will be critical ahead of the August 2026 transparency obligations.
3. **No formal implementing acts or delegated acts have been adopted yet.** The Commission has relied on voluntary codes of practice and soft-law guidelines rather than binding secondary legislation.
4. **Harmonized technical standards** are being developed by CEN-CENELEC JTC 21 under the Commission's standardization request (Art. 40(2)), but these are not yet finalized and are not implementing/delegated acts.
5. **Guidelines on high-risk classification** (Art. 6) were expected by February 2026 but have been delayed; a draft was released in May 2026.

question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite the most recent FDA guidance (e.g. Predetermined Change Control Plan finalised guidance, AI/ML SaMD action plan updates, life-cycle device guidance) and note the intersection with EU AI Act Annex I/Annex III where medical AI is classified as high-risk under the EU regime. Naive cannot answer with currency — FDA outputs are not in the knowledge base.

expected_citations.section: FDA AI/ML guidance + EU AI Act Annex I/III

output.answer:
# FDA AI/ML Medical Device Guidance & EU AI Act High-Risk Classification: Interaction Analysis

---

## 1. Most Recent FDA Guidance on AI/ML-Enabled Medical Devices

The FDA has issued two landmark guidance documents in close succession:

### A. Final Guidance: Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCP) — December 4, 2024
**Title:** *"Marketing Submission Recommendations for a Predetermined Change Control Plan for Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions"*

This finalized guidance introduces the **PCCP mechanism**, allowing manufacturers to **pre-authorize iterative algorithm updates** without filing a separate 510(k), De Novo, or PMA supplement for each change. Key elements:

- **Scope:** Applies to all AI-Enabled Device Software Functions (AI-DSFs) across all device classification pathways and AI components of combination products.
- **PCCP Components:** Manufacturers must specify (1) the planned modifications, (2) the modification protocol (including verification/validation), and (3) impact assessment on device safety/effectiveness.
- **Terminology shift:** The final guidance renamed "Machine Learning Device Software Functions (ML-DSFs)" to "AI-Enabled Device Software Functions (AI-DSFs)" to align with FDA's broader AI glossary.

*Source: [FDA PCCP Final Guidance](https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/software-medical-device-samd/artificial-intelligence-software-medical-device)*

### B. Draft Guidance: Lifecycle Management & Marketing Submissions — January 6–7, 2025
**Title:** *"Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions: Lifecycle Management and Marketing Submission Recommendations"*

This draft guidance applies a **Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC)** approach to AI-enabled devices, covering:

- **Lifecycle considerations:** Design, development, validation, deployment, and post-market surveillance specific to AI/ML.
- **Marketing submission recommendations:** What sponsors must include in FDA submissions for AI-enabled devices.
- **Transparency & labeling:** Recommendations for communicating AI functionality, limitations, and bias risks to users and patients.
- **Bias management:** Specific guidance on identifying and mitigating algorithmic bias across the device lifecycle.

*Source: [FDA AI-DSF Draft Guidance](https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/software-medical-device-samd/artificial-intelligence-software-medical-device); [DLA Piper analysis](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/2025/01/fda-issues-artificial-intelligence-enabled-device-software-functions-guidance)*

---

## 2. EU AI Act: High-Risk Classification for Medical AI

Under the **EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)**, AI systems used in medical devices are classified as **high-risk** through **Article 6(1)**, which establishes a two-pronged test:

| Condition | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| **(a)** | The AI system is intended to be used as a **safety component of a product**, or **is itself a product**, covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in **Annex II** (which includes the Medical Device Regulation (EU) 2017/745 and the IVD Regulation (EU) 2017/746). |
| **(b)** | The product is required to undergo a **third-party conformity assessment** (i.e., by a notified body) under that legislation. |

**Practical implication:** Virtually all Class IIa+ AI-enabled medical devices (and most AI-DSFs) that require notified-body involvement under the MDR/IVDR are automatically **high-risk** under the EU AI Act. This includes AI diagnostic software, AI-powered insulin pump controllers, and similar systems.

*Source: EU AI Act, Art. 6(1), Annex II; Recital 30*

### Cascade of Compliance Requirements (Title III, Chapter 2)

Once classified as high-risk, the AI system must comply with the following mandatory requirements:

| Requirement | Article | Key Obligations |
|-------------|---------|-----------------|
| **Risk Management System** | Art. 9 | Continuous, iterative process throughout the entire lifecycle; identify, estimate, evaluate, and mitigate known/foreseeable risks |
| **Data & Data Governance** | Art. 10 | Training/validation/testing datasets must be relevant, representative, free of errors, complete; bias examination; data governance practices including collection, labelling, filtration |
| **Technical Documentation** | Art. 11 | Comprehensive documentation (per Annex IV) demonstrating compliance; must be kept up-to-date |
| **Transparency & Information to Users** | Art. 13 | Clear information on capabilities, limitations, intended purpose, risks to fundamental rights |
| **Human Oversight** | Art. 14 | Systems must be designed so natural persons can effectively oversee functioning; measures built into the system or identified for deployers to implement |
| **Accuracy, Robustness & Cybersecurity** | Art. 15 | Appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and resilience; resistance to errors and adversarial attacks |
| **Quality Management System** | Art. 17 | Documented QMS covering regulatory compliance strategy, design controls, data management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting |
| **Post-Market Monitoring** | Art. 72 | Active, systematic collection and analysis of performance data throughout the system's lifetime; integrated with MDR post-market surveillance where applicable |

---

## 3. How the FDA Guidance and EU AI Act Interact

### A. Converging Principles, Diverging Mechanisms

| Dimension | FDA Approach | EU AI Act Approach | Convergence / Gap |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Lifecycle risk management** | TPLC approach (Jan 2025 draft guidance); PCCP for iterative updates | Art. 9: Continuous iterative risk management throughout lifecycle | ✅ **Strong convergence** — both mandate lifecycle-spanning risk management |
| **Post-market monitoring** | PCCP enables pre-authorized updates with ongoing monitoring; existing MDR requirements | Art. 72: Mandatory post-market monitoring system integrated with MDR/IVDR surveillance | ✅ **Convergence** — both require systematic post-market data collection; EU explicitly integrates with MDR |
| **Data quality & bias** | Jan 2025 draft guidance addresses bias identification and mitigation | Art. 10: Detailed data governance requirements (representativeness, bias examination, completeness) | ⚠️ **Partial convergence** — EU AI Act is more prescriptive on data governance (specific criteria for representativeness, statistical properties, geographical/contextual settings) |
| **Transparency** | Draft guidance recommends transparency labeling for AI functionality and limitations | Art. 13: Mandatory transparency obligations including fundamental rights risks | ⚠️ **Partial convergence** — EU goes further by requiring disclosure of fundamental rights risks and discrimination potential |
| **Human oversight** | FDA guidance references clinician oversight but is less prescriptive | Art. 14: Mandatory design-for-oversight; specific measures must be built into the system or identified for deployers | ❌ **Gap** — EU AI Act imposes far more specific and binding human oversight requirements |
| **Change management** | PCCP (Dec 2024 final guidance): Pre-authorized algorithm modifications without resubmission | Art. 17(a): QMS must include "procedures for the management of modifications"; changes may trigger re-assessment | ⚠️ **Tension** — FDA's PCCP streamlines changes; EU may require re-assessment under conformity procedures. A PCCP accepted by FDA does **not** automatically satisfy EU modification management requirements |
| **Conformity assessment** | FDA pre-market review (510(k)/De Novo/PMA) | Third-party conformity assessment by notified body + CE marking (Art. 49) | ❌ **Separate processes** — no mutual recognition; dual-market manufacturers must satisfy both |

### B. Key Areas of Regulatory Friction

1. **PCCP vs. EU Conformity Re-Assessment:** The FDA's PCCP mechanism allows pre-specified algorithm changes without new submissions. However, under the EU AI Act, modifications to a high-risk AI system may require **re-assessment by the notified body** if they affect compliance with the essential requirements (Art. 17(a) requires "procedures for the management of modifications"). A manufacturer cannot assume that a PCCP approved by the FDA will be accepted in the EU without separate conformity evaluation.

2. **Data Governance Granularity:** The EU AI Act's Article 10 imposes **specific, auditable criteria** for training data (representativeness, statistical properties, geographical/contextual relevance, bias detection protocols). The FDA's guidance addresses bias but is less granular. Manufacturers targeting both markets should align to the **higher bar** (EU Art. 10).

3. **Human Oversight Design Requirements:** The EU AI Act's Article 14 requires that human oversight measures be **designed into the system** or explicitly identified for deployers, with specific capabilities (understanding system limitations, detecting anomalies, overriding/ interrupting the system). The FDA's guidance is less prescriptive on this point. Dual-market devices should incorporate Art. 14-compliant oversight mechanisms.

4. **Fundamental Rights Dimension:** The EU AI Act uniquely requires assessment of risks to **fundamental rights** (non-discrimination, dignity, privacy) in addition to health/safety risks (Art. 9(2)(a), Art. 13). The FDA framework focuses on safety and effectiveness. This is an **additive EU requirement** with no FDA equivalent.

5. **Post-Market Integration with MDR/IVDR:** The EU AI Act explicitly requires that post-market monitoring under Art. 72 be **integrated** with the existing MDR/IVDR post-market surveillance system (Art. 72(4)). This creates a unified EU monitoring obligation that overlays on top of MDR requirements, whereas FDA post-market obligations operate under a separate regime.

### C. Strategic Implications for Dual-Market Manufacturers

| Strategy | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| **Design to the higher standard** | Align data governance, human oversight, and transparency practices to EU AI Act requirements (Arts. 10, 13, 14) — these generally exceed FDA guidance |
| **Harmonize lifecycle risk management** | Both frameworks converge on TPLC/continuous risk management; a unified risk management file can serve both, with EU-specific addenda for fundamental rights |
| **Separate change control tracks** | Maintain a PCCP for FDA submissions and a parallel EU modification management procedure under Art. 17(a); do not assume PCCP approval transfers to EU |
| **Integrate post-market systems** | Build a single post-market monitoring system that satisfies both FDA post-market surveillance requirements and EU AI Act Art. 72 / MDR Art. 84–86 |
| **Prepare for EU AI Act deadlines** | High-risk AI system obligations apply from **August 2, 2026**; plan conformity assessments now |

---

## Summary

The FDA's most recent guidance (PCCP final guidance, Dec 2024; AI-DSF lifecycle draft guidance, Jan 2025) and the EU AI Act's high-risk medical AI requirements (Art. 6, 8–17, 72) share a **common philosophy** — lifecycle risk management, post-market monitoring, and transparency — but differ significantly in **prescriptiveness and scope**. The EU AI Act imposes more granular data governance, stronger human oversight design requirements, a fundamental rights dimension absent from FDA guidance, and a conformity assessment regime that does not recognize FDA approvals. The most critical friction point is **change management**: FDA's PCCP streamlines iterative updates, while the EU may require re-assessment for the same changes. Dual-market manufacturers should design to the EU's higher bar while maintaining parallel regulatory tracks for each jurisdiction.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-002 (Training Data Management and Curation) against HIPAA's Business Associate Agreement requirements, what specific elements are MISSING from the SOP?

output.answer:
Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile a comprehensive gap analysis comparing the HIPAA BAA requirements against SOP-AIML-002.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-AIML-002 vs. HIPAA Business Associate Agreement Requirements

### What the SOP Currently Says

Section 6.5 ("Business Associate Agreements") contains the SOP's entire treatment of BAAs — a single brief paragraph:

> *"Where training data is sourced from or involves HIPAA-covered entities, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) shall be in place prior to data receipt. The Legal department maintains BAA records. The AI/ML Lead shall verify BAA coverage exists before initiating data transfer from a covered entity source. Any data sharing with vendors or partners involving PHI requires a downstream BAA executed by the Legal department."*

The RACI matrix (Section 3.1) assigns BAA Validation to the CISO (Responsible), CPO/DPO (Accountable), and CCO (Responsible), but provides no further procedural detail.

### Required BAA Elements Under HIPAA

HIPAA mandates specific contract provisions across two rules:

- **Privacy Rule** — 45 CFR §164.504(e)(2): Ten required contract provisions (items A through J) plus a termination authorization clause
- **Security Rule** — 45 CFR §164.314(a)(2)(i): Three required contract provisions (compliance, subcontractor flow-down, security incident reporting)
- **Breach Notification Rule** — 45 CFR §164.410: Timeliness and content requirements for breach notification by a business associate

---

### Specific Missing Elements

| # | Required BAA Element | HIPAA Citation | SOP Status | Gap Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Permitted and required uses/disclosures of PHI** | §164.504(e)(2)(i) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to establish the specific permitted and required uses and disclosures of PHI by the business associate. For AI/ML training data, this is critical — the BAA must define whether PHI may be used for model training, what types of models, and under what constraints. |
| **2** | **Prohibition on unauthorized use/disclosure** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(A) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the business associate will not use or further disclose PHI other than as permitted by the contract or required by law. This is foundational to HIPAA compliance. |
| **3** | **Appropriate safeguards to prevent unauthorized use/disclosure** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(B) | **PARTIAL** | The SOP has robust safeguards in Section 6 (access controls, encryption, audit logging), but it does not require these safeguards to be *reflected in or mandated by the BAA*. The BAA must contractually require the BA to use appropriate safeguards. |
| **4** | **Reporting of unauthorized use or disclosure** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(C) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the BA must report to the covered entity any use or disclosure of PHI not provided for by the contract. This is distinct from breach reporting — it covers *any* unauthorized use/disclosure, not just breaches of unsecured PHI. |
| **5** | **Subcontractor flow-down of same restrictions** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(D) | **PARTIAL** | The SOP mentions "downstream BAA" for vendors/partners, but does not require the BAA to include a provision ensuring that *any subcontractor* that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits PHI agrees to the *same restrictions and conditions* that apply to the BA. The downstream BAA reference is vague and doesn't specify that identical restrictions must flow down. |
| **6** | **Individual access rights (§164.524)** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(E) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the BA will make PHI available to individuals for access requests under §164.524. This is especially important for training data — individuals may request access to their PHI even when it resides in training datasets. |
| **7** | **Amendment rights (§164.526)** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(F) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the BA will make PHI available for amendment and incorporate amendments. If a patient's data in a training set is inaccurate, the BAA must provide a mechanism for correction. |
| **8** | **Accounting of disclosures (§164.528)** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(G) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the BA will make available the information required to provide an accounting of disclosures. Training data may be disclosed for research, validation, or other purposes — the BAA must support accounting for these disclosures. |
| **9** | **Compliance with covered entity's Privacy Rule obligations** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(H) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that, to the extent the BA carries out a covered entity's obligation under the Privacy Rule, the BA will comply with the requirements that apply to the covered entity. |
| **10** | **Access to internal practices by HHS Secretary** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(I) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision making the BA's internal practices, books, and records relating to the use and disclosure of PHI available to the HHS Secretary for compliance determination. This is a mandatory BAA term. |
| **11** | **Return or destruction of PHI at contract termination** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(J) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision for the return or destruction of all PHI at contract termination (or, if infeasible, extension of protections). This is a **critical gap** for AI/ML training data — PHI may persist in trained model weights, embeddings, or cached datasets, and the SOP provides no guidance on how "return or destroy" applies to these derived artifacts. |
| **12** | **Termination authorization for material breach** | §164.504(e)(2)(iii) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to authorize the covered entity to terminate the contract if it determines the BA has violated a material term. This is a required BAA provision that gives the covered entity enforcement leverage. |
| **13** | **Security Rule compliance** | §164.314(a)(2)(i)(A) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision that the BA will comply with the applicable requirements of the Security Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart C). While the SOP has security controls, the BAA must *contractually* bind the BA to Security Rule compliance. |
| **14** | **Security incident and breach reporting** | §164.314(a)(2)(i)(C) and §164.410 | **MISSING** | The SOP does not require the BAA to include a provision for reporting security incidents and breaches of unsecured PHI to the covered entity. Specifically, §164.410 requires the BA to notify the covered entity without unreasonable delay and no later than **60 calendar days** after discovery, including identification of each affected individual. The SOP's emergency procedures (Section 8.4) address internal incident response but do not address BAA-specific breach notification obligations to covered entities. |
| **15** | **Permitted uses for BA's own management/administration** | §164.504(e)(4)(i) | **MISSING** | The SOP does not address the BAA's optional provision permitting the BA to use PHI for its own proper management and administration or to provide data aggregation services. While optional, this is a standard BAA provision that should be addressed — either included or explicitly excluded. |
| **16** | **Minimum necessary standard in BAA context** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii) (implicit) | **PARTIAL** | The SOP has a data minimization policy (Section 4.6), but does not tie this to the BAA's requirement that uses/disclosures be limited to the minimum necessary. The BAA should reflect minimum necessary restrictions specific to the PHI being shared. |
| **17** | **BAA content verification checklist** | Implied by all above | **MISSING** | The SOP states the AI/ML Lead shall "verify BAA coverage exists" but provides no checklist, template, or criteria for what constitutes an adequate BAA. There is no verification that the BAA contains all required HIPAA provisions. |
| **18** | **PHI in derived artifacts (model weights, embeddings)** | §164.504(e)(2)(ii)(J) and (A) | **MISSING** | A unique AI/ML concern not addressed: when PHI is used to train models, the resulting model weights, parameters, or embeddings may constitute PHI or derivatives. The SOP does not address how BAA obligations (use restrictions, return/destruction, safeguards) apply to these derived artifacts. |

---

### Summary of Findings

The SOP's BAA section (Section 6.5) is essentially a **placeholder** — it acknowledges that BAAs must exist and assigns record-keeping to Legal, but it contains **none of the 10 required Privacy Rule contract provisions** (§164.504(e)(2)(ii)(A)–(J)), **none of the 3 required Security Rule contract provisions** (§164.314(a)(2)(i)(A)–(C)), and **no breach notification requirements** (§164.410). The most consequential gaps for an AI/ML training data context are:

1. **Return/destruction of PHI at termination** (Gap #11) — especially critical because training data PHI may be embedded in model weights, synthetic data generators, or cached pipelines with no clear destruction path.
2. **Individual rights provisions** (Gaps #6, #7, #8) — patients retain access, amendment, and accounting rights over their PHI even when it's in training datasets.
3. **Unauthorized use/disclosure reporting** (Gap #4) — the BAA must require the BA to report *any* use outside the contract, not just breaches.
4. **PHI in derived artifacts** (Gap #18) — a novel AI/ML risk where model memorization or extraction attacks could constitute unauthorized disclosure of PHI that originated under a BAA.

Compliance level: gap

# Gap Analysis: SOP-FIN-001 vs. NIST AI RMF GOVERN & MAP Functions

## Executive Summary

SOP-FIN-001 (v3.6) is a mature, SR 11-7–centric model risk governance document that has been progressively updated to incorporate EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF references. However, while the SOP **names** NIST AI RMF in §1.3 and the revision history (v3.0) claims to "introduce AI risk categories (GOVERN, MAP)," the actual procedural content remains heavily weighted toward traditional model risk management (validation, monitoring, tiering). The NIST AI RMF's distinctive requirements—particularly around **diversity, stakeholder engagement, societal impact characterization, and third-party/supply-chain risk**—are largely absent.

---

## GOVERN Function Gaps

### GOVERN 1 — Policies, processes, procedures, and practices

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 1.1** – Legal/regulatory requirements understood, managed, documented | §1.3 lists applicable regulations | ⚠️ **Partial** — Regulations are *listed* but there is no process for *tracking evolving* legal requirements or documenting how each requirement is operationalized. |
| **GOVERN 1.2** – Trustworthy AI characteristics integrated into policies | §4.2 (fairness), §4.3 (explainability), §6.2 (accuracy/robustness) | ⚠️ **Partial** — Only fairness, explainability, and accuracy are explicitly integrated. **Missing explicit integration of: safety, security & resilience, accountability & transparency, and privacy-enhancement** as trustworthiness characteristics in governance policies. |
| **GOVERN 1.3** – Risk management activities based on organizational risk tolerance | §4.4 defines risk tiers; §1.1 references "Board's risk appetite" | ⚠️ **Partial** — Risk tiers exist but the organization's **overall AI risk tolerance/appetite is never defined or documented**. Tier assignment criteria are materiality/complexity-based, not tolerance-based. |
| **GOVERN 1.4** – Risk management process established through transparent mechanisms | §5 detailed procedures | ✅ Covered |
| **GOVERN 1.5** – Ongoing monitoring and periodic review with defined frequency | §7.1–7.3 | ✅ Covered |
| **GOVERN 1.6** – AI system inventory mechanisms resourced by risk priority | §5.2 MRMS inventory | ✅ Covered |
| **GOVERN 1.7** – Decommissioning processes that don't increase risk | §5.1.5 Phase V | ✅ Covered |

### GOVERN 2 — Accountability structures

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 2.1** – Roles, responsibilities, lines of communication documented and clear | §3.1 RACI matrix, §3.2 role details | ✅ Covered |
| **GOVERN 2.2** – Personnel and partners receive AI risk management training | §9 training table | ✅ Covered |
| **GOVERN 2.3** – Executive leadership takes responsibility for AI risk decisions | VP and CAIO are "Accountable" in RACI; Board Risk Committee receives quarterly reports | ⚠️ **Partial** — No explicit statement that **executive leadership (Board, CEO) takes ultimate responsibility** for AI system development and deployment risk decisions. Accountability stops at VP/CAIO level. |

### GOVERN 3 — Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility ❌ ENTIRELY MISSING

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 3.1** – Decision-making informed by demographically and disciplinarily diverse team | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No requirement that AI risk decision-making bodies include demographic or disciplinary diversity. The RACI matrix is limited to finance, compliance, and AI roles with no representation from affected communities, social scientists, ethicists, or end-user advocates. |
| **GOVERN 3.2** – Policies to define/differentiate roles for human-AI configurations and oversight | §4.5 human oversight for Tier A | ⚠️ **Partial** — Human oversight is defined for Tier A, but there is no policy differentiating who *configures* the human-AI interaction vs. who *oversees* it, and no broader framework for human-AI role delineation across all tiers. |

### GOVERN 4 — Culture that considers and communicates AI risk

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 4.1** – Practices fostering critical thinking and safety-first mindset | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No organizational practices to foster a critical thinking or safety-first mindset in AI design/development. The SOP is compliance-driven, not culture-driven. |
| **GOVERN 4.2** – Teams document risks/impacts and communicate broadly | Model Narrative & Validation Reports exist | ⚠️ **Partial** — Technical documentation exists, but there is **no requirement for broader communication** of AI risks and impacts beyond the immediate governance chain (Model Owner → VP → CAIO → Board Committee). No communication to affected communities, public transparency, or cross-organizational sharing. |
| **GOVERN 4.3** – Practices for AI testing, incident identification, and information sharing | §8.2 escalation; SOP-SEC-003 referenced | ⚠️ **Partial** — Incident identification and testing are covered, but **information sharing** (e.g., lessons learned across model teams, industry disclosure of AI incidents) is absent. |

### GOVERN 5 — Robust stakeholder engagement ❌ ENTIRELY MISSING

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 5.1** – Collect, consider, prioritize external stakeholder feedback on individual/societal impacts | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No mechanism to collect feedback from external stakeholders (affected borrowers, consumer advocacy groups, civil society organizations, community representatives) regarding AI risk impacts. |
| **GOVERN 5.2** – Incorporate adjudicated stakeholder feedback into system design | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No process for feeding external stakeholder input back into model design or implementation decisions. |

### GOVERN 6 — Third-party and supply-chain risk ❌ ENTIRELY MISSING

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 6.1** – Policies addressing third-party AI risks, including IP infringement | §1.2 mentions vendor/open-source models are in scope | ❌ **MISSING** — Despite stating vendor models are in scope, the SOP contains **zero specific policies or procedures** addressing third-party AI risk, IP infringement risk, or supply-chain due diligence. No vendor risk assessment, no contractual requirements, no IP clearance process. |
| **GOVERN 6.2** – Contingency processes for third-party data/AI system failures | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No contingency plan for vendor model failure, third-party data supply disruption, or open-source dependency risks. |

---

## MAP Function Gaps

### MAP 1 — Context is established and understood

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP 1.1** – Intended purposes, beneficial uses, laws/norms, settings, user expectations, positive/negative impacts, assumptions/limitations documented | §5.1.1 business case; §5.3 Model Narrative §1 (Intended Use) | ⚠️ **Partial** — Intended purpose and data sources are documented, but **missing**: (a) potentially beneficial uses beyond the primary purpose; (b) context-specific norms and expectations; (c) systematic documentation of positive/negative impacts to individuals, communities, organizations, and society; (d) explicit assumptions and limitations tied to TEVV considerations. |
| **MAP 1.2** – Interdisciplinary, demographically diverse actors participate in context-setting | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No requirement for interdisciplinary or demographically diverse participation when establishing context for a model. |
| **MAP 1.3** – Organization's mission and goals for AI technology documented | §1.1 references ethical AI commitment | ⚠️ **Partial** — High-level mission statement exists but no per-model documentation connecting the specific AI system to organizational mission and AI technology goals. |
| **MAP 1.4** – Business value/context clearly defined or re-evaluated | §5.1.1 business case | ✅ Covered |
| **MAP 1.5** – Organizational risk tolerances determined and documented | §4.4 risk tiers | ⚠️ **Partial** — Risk tiers are defined but **organizational risk tolerances** (quantitative or qualitative statements of acceptable risk) are not documented. |
| **MAP 1.6** – System requirements elicited from relevant AI actors; socio-technical implications considered | Table 5-A (performance), Table 5-B (NFRs) | ⚠️ **Partial** — Technical requirements exist but are not elicited from a broad set of AI actors. **Socio-technical implications are not considered** in design decisions. |

### MAP 2 — Categorization of the AI system

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP 2.1** – Specific tasks and methods defined | §1.2 model types; §5.3 §3 algorithm selection | ✅ Covered |
| **MAP 2.2** – Knowledge limits and human oversight of output documented | §5.3 §5 "known failure modes"; §4.5 human oversight | ⚠️ **Partial** — Failure modes are documented, but **explicit knowledge limits** (what the system cannot know or reliably predict) are not required. Documentation of how output is overseen by humans is limited to Tier A. |
| **MAP 2.3** – Scientific integrity and TEVV considerations documented (experimental design, data selection, construct validation) | §5.1.2–5.1.3 development and validation | ⚠️ **Partial** — Data quality and validation are covered, but **scientific integrity** as a distinct requirement and **construct validation** (are we measuring what we intend to measure?) are not explicitly addressed. |

### MAP 3 — AI capabilities, targeted usage, goals, benefits, and costs

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP 3.1** – Potential benefits examined and documented | §5.1.1 business case (problem statement only) | ⚠️ **Partial** — Problem statements exist but **potential benefits are not explicitly examined or documented**. |
| **MAP 3.2** – Potential costs (including non-monetary) from errors or trustworthiness failures documented | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No requirement to document potential costs of AI errors, including non-monetary costs such as harm to individuals, reputational damage, societal impacts, or loss of trust. |
| **MAP 3.3** – Targeted application scope specified and documented | §1.2 scope; §5.3 §5 implementation guide | ✅ Covered |
| **MAP 3.4** – Operator/practitioner proficiency with AI system assessed; technical standards/certifications defined | §9 training | ⚠️ **Partial** — Training is required but **proficiency assessment** (verifying operators can effectively oversee the AI system) and **technical standards/certifications** are not addressed. |
| **MAP 3.5** – Human oversight processes defined per GOVERN policies | §4.5 | ✅ Covered |

### MAP 4 — Risks and benefits mapped for all components including third-party ❌ LARGELY MISSING

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP 4.1** – Approaches for mapping technology and legal risks of components, including third-party data/software and IP risks | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No approach for mapping risks of AI system components, including third-party data, software, or IP infringement risks. |
| **MAP 4.2** – Internal risk controls for third-party AI technologies identified and documented | §6.3 access control (internal artifacts only) | ❌ **MISSING** — No identification or documentation of internal risk controls specifically for third-party AI components. |

### MAP 5 — Impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society ❌ LARGELY MISSING

| Subcategory | SOP Coverage | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP 5.1** – Likelihood and magnitude of each impact (beneficial and harmful) documented, based on expected use, past uses, incident reports, external feedback | §5.1.3 bias audit (partial) | ❌ **MISSING** — The bias audit addresses fairness impacts for protected demographics but does **not** systematically characterize the **likelihood and magnitude** of broader impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society. No use of past incidents, external feedback, or comparable system data. |
| **MAP 5.2** – Practices and personnel for regular engagement with relevant AI actors and integrating feedback on positive, negative, and unanticipated impacts | **No mention** | ❌ **MISSING** — No practices for ongoing engagement with external AI actors or integrating their feedback on impacts. |

---

## Summary of Critical Gaps

The following elements are **entirely missing** from SOP-FIN-001 and represent the most significant deviations from NIST AI RMF GOVERN and MAP:

| # | Missing Element | NIST AI RMF Reference | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion & accessibility in AI risk decision-making** | GOVERN 3.1 | Homogeneous teams may miss blind spots in bias, fairness, and societal impact |
| 2 | **External stakeholder engagement and feedback integration** | GOVERN 5.1, 5.2; MAP 1.2, 5.2 | Affected communities have no voice; risks of unintended harm go undetected |
| 3 | **Third-party/supply-chain AI risk policies and contingency processes** | GOVERN 6.1, 6.2; MAP 4.1, 4.2 | Vendor models are in scope but have zero governance controls; IP and supply-chain risks unaddressed |
| 4 | **Systematic characterization of societal impacts (likelihood, magnitude, beneficial/harmful)** | MAP 5.1 | No structured assessment of how credit scoring models affect communities and society |
| 5 | **Documentation of non-monetary costs of AI errors and trustworthiness failures** | MAP 3.2 | Cost-benefit analysis is incomplete; harms to individuals are invisible in go/no-go decisions |
| 6 | **Organizational AI risk tolerance/appetite statement** | GOVERN 1.3; MAP 1.5 | Risk tiers are assigned without a defined tolerance baseline; tier decisions lack an anchor |
| 7 | **Critical thinking and safety-first culture practices** | GOVERN 4.1 | Compliance-driven approach without cultural reinforcement may lead to checkbox mentality |
| 8 | **Broader communication of AI risks and impacts** | GOVERN 4.2 | Risk information siloed within governance chain; no transparency to affected parties |
| 9 | **Executive leadership responsibility for AI risk decisions** | GOVERN 2.3 | Accountability stops at VP/CAIO; Board and C-suite are informed but not explicitly responsible |
| 10 | **Knowledge limits documentation and construct validation** | MAP 2.2, 2.3 | Models' epistemic boundaries are undocumented; risk of over-reliance on model outputs |

---

## Recommendations

1. **Add a GOVERN 3 section** requiring demographic and disciplinary diversity on model risk decision-making bodies (e.g., include ethicists, social scientists, consumer advocates on validation committees).
2. **Establish a stakeholder engagement program** (GOVERN 5 / MAP 5.2) with mechanisms for collecting and integrating feedback from affected borrowers and community organizations.
3. **Create a Third-Party AI Risk Policy** (GOVERN 6) covering vendor due diligence, contractual AI risk clauses, IP clearance, and contingency plans for vendor failure.
4. **Require an Impact Assessment Template** (MAP 5.1) that documents likelihood and magnitude of impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society for each Tier A/B model.
5. **Define and document organizational AI risk tolerance** (GOVERN 1.3 / MAP 1.5) as a quantitative or qualitative statement approved by the Board.
6. **Add a "Knowledge Limits" section** to the Model Narrative template (MAP 2.2) requiring explicit documentation of what the model cannot reliably predict.
7. **Insert an executive accountability clause** (GOVERN 2.3) stating that the Board and CEO bear ultimate responsibility for AI risk decisions.

Compliance level: partial

# Gap Analysis: SOP-CLIN-001 vs. EU AI Act Article 11 & Article 17

## Executive Summary

SOP-CLIN-001 is a well-structured clinical validation SOP with strong procedural rigor around data lockboxing, statistical analysis, and post-market monitoring. However, when measured against the **EU AI Act's Article 11 (Technical Documentation)** and **Article 17 (Quality Management System)** requirements, several significant gaps emerge. The SOP treats EU AI Act compliance as a documentation output (Section 5.5 mentions "Annex IV documentation elements") rather than embedding the Act's systemic requirements into its procedural framework.

---

## PART A: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 11 — TECHNICAL DOCUMENTATION

Article 11 requires that technical documentation be "drawn up before that system is placed on the market or put into service and shall be kept up-to-date," containing at minimum the elements of **Annex IV**. The SOP references Annex IV in Section 5.5 but does not operationalize most of its nine mandatory sections:

### Gap 1: No Procedure for Annex IV §1 — General Description of the AI System

**Requirement:** Annex IV §1 requires a general description including the AI system's intended purpose, the name of the provider, how the system interacts with other hardware/software, the versions of relevant software/firmware, and a description of the forms in which the system is placed on the market.

**What the SOP has:** Section 1.2 defines scope at a product-category level (e.g., "AI-driven patient risk scoring models"), but there is **no procedural step requiring the creation of a per-system general description document** capturing provider identity, system versioning, interaction architecture, or deployment form factor.

**What's missing:** A mandated deliverable (e.g., "System General Description Document") per CDSS that covers provider identity, intended purpose statement, system architecture and interaction diagrams, software/firmware versions, and deployment form.

---

### Gap 2: No Procedure for Annex IV §2 — Detailed Description of Design & Development

**Requirement:** Annex IV §2 requires detailed documentation of the development process, including design specifications, the system architecture, algorithms and data structures used, training methodology, design choices and trade-offs with justification, and the data governance measures per Article 10.

**What the SOP has:** Section 5.4 covers code freeze and containerization for the validation run, but this is a **snapshot at validation time**, not a development-phase documentation procedure. The SOP references SOP-DS-001 (Model Development and Coding Standard) but does not itself require or verify that design documentation is produced.

**What's missing:**
- A procedural gate requiring **design specification documentation** before validation begins
- Documentation of **algorithm architecture and data structures**
- Documentation of **design trade-offs and justifications** (e.g., why a specific model architecture was chosen)
- A procedure to verify that **Article 10 data governance** documentation (data provenance, representativeness, bias assessment at the data level) is complete and included in the technical file

---

### Gap 3: No Procedure for Annex IV §3 — Monitoring, Functioning, and Control

**Requirement:** Annex IV §3 requires detailed information on the monitoring, functioning, and control of the AI system, including its capabilities and limitations, the level of human oversight, and the mechanisms for logging.

**What the SOP has:** Section 7 covers post-market KPIs (drift, override rate, bias) and Section 6.1 covers access logs and container integrity. However, there is **no procedural requirement to document** the system's monitoring architecture, human oversight mechanisms, or logging capabilities as part of the technical documentation package.

**What's missing:**
- A mandated deliverable describing the **human oversight mechanisms** (how clinicians interact with, override, or are alerted by the CDSS) — the SOP assumes clinical primacy (Policy §1) but never documents the specific oversight design
- Documentation of **logging capabilities** beyond audit trail access logs — Article 12 requires automatic event logging during operation, and Annex IV §3 requires this to be described in the technical documentation
- A description of **system limitations and conditions under which performance may degrade**

---

### Gap 4: No Procedure for Annex IV §4 — Appropriateness of Performance Metrics

**Requirement:** Annex IV §4 requires a description of the appropriateness of the performance metrics for the specific AI system.

**What the SOP has:** Section 5.5 defines specific benchmarks (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, Brier score, etc.) and Section 5.1 requires a Statistical Analysis Plan. However, the SOP does not require a **justification of why these metrics are appropriate** for the specific clinical use case.

**What's missing:** A procedural step requiring the Lead Data Scientist and Biostatistician to **document the rationale for metric selection** — e.g., why AUC is appropriate vs. AUPRC for an imbalanced dataset, why the Brier score threshold of <0.10 is clinically meaningful, and how metrics relate to the specific risk profile of the CDSS.

---

### Gap 5: No Procedure for Annex IV §5 — Risk Management System Documentation

**Requirement:** Annex IV §5 requires a detailed description of the risk management system in accordance with Article 9.

**What the SOP has:** The SOP references SOP-CLIN-002 (CDSS Risk Management and FMEA Procedure) in Section 10.1, but **does not itself contain or mandate any risk management documentation step** within the validation workflow. The validation phases (5.1–5.6) proceed from study design to sign-off with no risk management gate.

**What's missing:**
- A procedural requirement that the **risk management documentation per Article 9** (identification, analysis, estimation, evaluation, and mitigation of risks) is complete and included in the technical documentation before the system is released
- A cross-reference gate ensuring SOP-CLIN-002 outputs are verified and attached to the FVR
- The Article 9 requirement for **continuous iterative risk management** throughout the lifecycle is only partially addressed by post-market monitoring (Section 7) but not explicitly linked to risk re-evaluation

---

### Gap 6: No Procedure for Annex IV §6 — Lifecycle Changes and Versioning

**Requirement:** Annex IV §6 requires a description of relevant changes made by the provider to the system through its lifecycle.

**What the SOP has:** Section 10.1 references SOP-CLIN-005 (Clinical Algorithm Change Management and Versioning), but the SOP itself has **no procedural step to maintain a change log** as part of the technical documentation. The revision history in Section 11 only tracks changes to the SOP itself, not to the CDSS.

**What's missing:** A mandated **system-level change log** as part of the technical documentation, documenting all modifications to the AI system (model updates, data pipeline changes, threshold adjustments) with dates, descriptions, and impact assessments.

---

### Gap 7: No Procedure for Annex IV §7 — Harmonised Standards Applied

**Requirement:** Annex IV §7 requires a list of harmonised standards applied in full or in part, and where none are applied, a description of how compliance is achieved.

**What the SOP has:** Section 10.2 lists external standards (IEC 62304, ISO 13485, NIST AI RMF) but there is **no procedural requirement to document which harmonised standards were applied** to each specific CDSS, nor to document the means of compliance where standards are not applied.

**What's missing:** A procedural step requiring the Regulatory Affairs Specialist to **document the harmonised standards applied** (or the alternative compliance means) for each CDSS in the technical documentation file.

---

### Gap 8: No Procedure for Annex IV §8 — EU Declaration of Conformity

**Requirement:** Annex IV §8 requires a copy of the EU declaration of conformity.

**What the SOP has:** The SOP has a sign-off gate (Section 5.6) with a "Deployment Readiness Checklist," but there is **no mention of an EU declaration of conformity** or any procedure for drawing one up.

**What's missing:** A procedural step requiring the Regulatory Affairs Specialist to **draw up and attach the EU declaration of conformity** (per Article 47) to the technical documentation before market placement.

---

### Gap 9: No Procedure for Annex IV §9 — Post-Market Monitoring Plan

**Requirement:** Annex IV §9 requires a detailed description of the system in place to evaluate the AI system performance in the post-market phase in accordance with Article 72, including the post-market monitoring plan.

**What the SOP has:** Section 7.1 requires a PMCF plan filed within 30 days of deployment. However, this is **not explicitly structured as an Annex IV §9 deliverable**, and there is no requirement that this plan be included in the technical documentation package.

**What's missing:** A procedural requirement that the **post-market monitoring plan** is part of the technical documentation (not just a standalone filing), and that it is updated as part of the "kept up-to-date" obligation under Article 11(1).

---

### Gap 10: No Procedure for Keeping Technical Documentation Up-to-Date

**Requirement:** Article 11(1) requires that technical documentation "shall be kept up-to-date."

**What the SOP has:** The SOP has an annual re-validation cycle (Section 7.3) and a document review cycle, but there is **no explicit procedure for updating the technical documentation** when changes occur between review cycles (e.g., model retraining, data pipeline changes, new bias findings).

**What's missing:** A procedure mandating that the technical documentation is **reviewed and updated upon any material change** to the CDSS, not just on the annual cycle.

---

## PART B: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 17 — QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM

Article 17 requires providers to put a QMS in place that is "documented in a systematic and orderly manner in the form of written policies, procedures and instructions," covering at minimum the aspects listed in Article 17(1)(a)–(m).

### Gap 11: No Strategy for Regulatory Compliance Including Conformity Assessment [Art. 17(1)(a)]

**Requirement:** A strategy for regulatory compliance, including compliance with conformity assessment procedures and procedures for the management of modifications to the high-risk AI system.

**What the SOP has:** Section 4 Policy §6 states alignment with the EU AI Act, and Section 3 assigns a Regulatory Affairs Specialist as "Consulted." But there is **no documented strategy** for how conformity assessment (Article 43) will be conducted, which assessment procedure applies, or how modifications trigger re-assessment.

**What's missing:**
- A documented **conformity assessment strategy** (e.g., Annex III-based self-assessment vs. third-party notified body assessment)
- A **modification management procedure** that defines when a change triggers re-assessment — SOP-CLIN-005 is referenced but not integrated into this SOP's validation gates

---

### Gap 12: No Design Control and Design Verification Procedures [Art. 17(1)(b)]

**Requirement:** Techniques, procedures and systematic actions to be used for the design, design control and design verification of the high-risk AI system.

**What the SOP has:** The SOP covers validation (Phases 0–5) but **not design control or design verification**. Design verification (confirming the system was built as specified) is distinct from validation (confirming the system meets clinical needs), and the SOP conflates the two.

**What's missing:** Design control procedures (design inputs → outputs → reviews → verification → validation) as required by both Article 17(1)(b) and IEC 62304.

---

### Gap 13: No Development Quality Control and Quality Assurance Procedures [Art. 17(1)(c)]

**Requirement:** Techniques, procedures and systematic actions to be used for the development, quality control and quality assurance of the high-risk AI system.

**What the SOP has:** The SOP focuses on validation execution, not on **development-phase QC/QA**. Code review, unit testing, integration testing, and model development quality gates are not addressed.

**What's missing:** Development-phase quality control procedures, or an explicit cross-reference to SOP-DS-001 with a verification gate confirming that development QC was completed before validation begins.

---

### Gap 14: No Examination/Test/Validation Procedures Before and During Development [Art. 17(1)(d)]

**Requirement:** Examination, test and validation procedures to be carried out before, during and after the development of the high-risk AI system, and the frequency with which they have to be carried out.

**What the SOP has:** Section 5 covers validation *after* development (Phases 0–5). There are **no procedures for testing before or during development** (e.g., unit tests, integration tests, smoke tests during model training).

**What's missing:** A procedure or cross-reference requiring documented testing at each development stage, with defined frequencies.

---

### Gap 15: No Technical Specifications and Standards Application Procedure [Art. 17(1)(e)]

**Requirement:** Technical specifications, including standards, to be applied and, where harmonised standards are not applied in full, the means to ensure compliance.

**What the SOP has:** Section 10.2 lists standards but does not require **documentation of which standards apply to each CDSS** or how compliance is achieved where standards are not fully applied.

**What's missing:** A per-CDSS standards mapping and gap analysis, documenting which harmonised standards apply and how unmet requirements are addressed.

---

### Gap 16: Incomplete Data Management Procedures [Art. 17(1)(f)]

**Requirement:** Systems and procedures for data management, including data acquisition, collection, analysis, labelling, storage, filtration, mining, aggregation, retention, and any other operation regarding data performed before placing on the market.

**What the SOP has:** Section 5.2 covers data acquisition, de-identification, and hold-out set creation. Section 5.3 covers labelling (Gold Standard Annotation Guide). However, several data management operations are **not addressed**: data filtration, data mining, data aggregation, and **data retention policies**.

**What's missing:**
- **Data retention policies** — how long are training, validation, and test datasets retained? (Article 11 requires documentation to be kept up-to-date; data retention is a QMS element)
- **Data filtration and mining procedures** — how are features selected, filtered, or mined from the raw data?
- **Data aggregation procedures** — how are data from multiple sources combined?

---

### Gap 17: Risk Management System Not Integrated [Art. 17(1)(g)]

**Requirement:** The risk management system referred to in Article 9.

**What the SOP has:** SOP-CLIN-002 is referenced but **not integrated** into the validation workflow. There is no gate requiring risk management documentation to be complete before validation proceeds.

**What's missing:** A procedural gate in the validation workflow (e.g., between Phase 0 and Phase 1) confirming that the Article 9 risk management system is established, documented, and current.

---

### Gap 18: Post-Market Monitoring System Not Fully Aligned with Article 72 [Art. 17(1)(h)]

**Requirement:** The setting-up, implementation and maintenance of a post-market monitoring system, in accordance with Article 72.

**What the SOP has:** Section 7 covers post-market KPIs and reporting cadence. However, the SOP does not reference **Article 72** specifically, and the PMCF plan (Section 7.1) is filed as a standalone document rather than as part of the QMS.

**What's missing:** Explicit alignment with Article 72's requirements for the post-market monitoring system, including systematic collection and review of experience from use, and integration of the monitoring system into the QMS documentation.

---

### Gap 19: No Serious Incident Reporting Procedure [Art. 17(1)(i)]

**Requirement:** Procedures related to the reporting of a serious incident in accordance with Article 73.

**What the SOP has:** Section 8.2 covers escalation for critical validation failures and post-deployment safety events, including RCA within 14 days and reporting to regulatory bodies. However, this is **not specifically aligned with Article 73's serious incident reporting requirements**, which have specific timelines and reporting obligations to market surveillance authorities.

**What's missing:**
- A procedure specifically addressing **Article 73 serious incident reporting** (distinct from general escalation)
- Defined **reporting timelines** aligned with Article 73 (e.g., immediately after becoming aware, with a detailed report within a specified period)
- A procedure for reporting to the **market surveillance authorities** of the Member States where the AI system is made available

---

### Gap 20: No Communication Procedure with National Competent Authorities [Art. 17(1)(j)]

**Requirement:** The handling of communication with national competent authorities, other relevant authorities, notified bodies, other operators, customers, or other interested parties.

**What the SOP has:** The Regulatory Affairs Specialist is listed as "Consulted" in Section 3, and Section 8.2 mentions reporting to regulatory bodies. However, there is **no documented procedure** for ongoing communication with national competent authorities, notified bodies, or other interested parties.

**What's missing:** A communication procedure defining who is responsible for liaising with which authorities, the escalation paths, and the documentation of all communications.

---

### Gap 21: No Resource Management Procedure [Art. 17(1)(l)]

**Requirement:** Resource management, including security-of-supply related measures.

**What the SOP has:** The VP of Clinical AI Products is responsible for "resource allocation for validation programs" (Section 3), but there is **no documented resource management procedure** covering staffing, infrastructure, or supply-chain continuity.

**What's missing:** A resource management procedure addressing staffing adequacy, computational infrastructure, and supply-chain considerations for the AI system's lifecycle.

---

### Gap 22: No Accountability Framework [Art. 17(1)(m)]

**Requirement:** An accountability framework setting out the responsibilities of the management and other staff with regard to all aspects listed in Article 17(1).

**What the SOP has:** Section 3 provides a RACI matrix for validation activities only. This is **not an accountability framework** covering all QMS aspects (design, development, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, communication, record-keeping, resource management).

**What's missing:** An expanded accountability framework covering all 13 aspects of Article 17(1), not just validation.

---

## Summary Table

| # | Gap | EU AI Act Reference | SOP Section Affected | Severity |
|---|-----|---------------------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | No procedure for Annex IV §1 general description | Art. 11 / Annex IV §1 | §5 (new gate needed) | High |
| 2 | No procedure for Annex IV §2 design & development documentation | Art. 11 / Annex IV §2 | §5 (new gate needed) | High |
| 3 | No procedure for Annex IV §3 monitoring, functioning, control | Art. 11 / Annex IV §3 | §5, §7 | High |
| 4 | No justification of performance metric appropriateness | Art. 11 / Annex IV §4 | §5.5 | Medium |
| 5 | Risk management documentation not integrated into validation workflow | Art. 11 / Annex IV §5 / Art. 9 | §5 (new gate needed) | High |
| 6 | No system-level lifecycle change log in technical documentation | Art. 11 / Annex IV §6 | §5, §10 | Medium |
| 7 | No harmonised standards mapping per CDSS | Art. 11 / Annex IV §7 | §5, §10 | Medium |
| 8 | No EU declaration of conformity procedure | Art. 11 / Annex IV §8 / Art. 47 | §5.6 | High |
| 9 | Post-market monitoring plan not part of technical documentation | Art. 11 / Annex IV §9 / Art. 72 | §7.1 | Medium |
| 10 | No procedure for keeping technical documentation up-to-date | Art. 11(1) | §7.3 | High |
| 11 | No conformity assessment strategy or modification management | Art. 17(1)(a) | §4, §5 | High |
| 12 | No design control or design verification procedures | Art. 17(1)(b) | §5 (new phase needed) | High |
| 13 | No development QC/QA procedures | Art. 17(1)(c) | §5 (new phase needed) | High |
| 14 | No testing procedures before/during development | Art. 17(1)(d) | §5 (new phase needed) | High |
| 15 | No per-CDSS standards mapping and gap analysis | Art. 17(1)(e) | §10 | Medium |
| 16 | Incomplete data management (no retention, filtration, mining, aggregation) | Art. 17(1)(f) | §5.2 | Medium |
| 17 | Risk management system not integrated as QMS element | Art. 17(1)(g) / Art. 9 | §5 (new gate needed) | High |
| 18 | Post-market monitoring not aligned with Article 72 | Art. 17(1)(h) / Art. 72 | §7 | Medium |
| 19 | No Article 73 serious incident reporting procedure | Art. 17(1)(i) / Art. 73 | §8 | High |
| 20 | No communication procedure with national competent authorities | Art. 17(1)(j) | §3, §8 | Medium |
| 21 | No resource management procedure | Art. 17(1)(l) | §3 | Low |
| 22 | No accountability framework covering all QMS aspects | Art. 17(1)(m) | §3 | Medium |

---

## Key Recommendations

1. **Expand the validation workflow** (Section 5) to include pre-validation gates that verify the existence and completeness of all Annex IV documentation sections before Phase 3 execution begins.
2. **Create a Technical Documentation Template** aligned with all 9 sections of Annex IV, and mandate its completion as a Phase 0 deliverable.
3. **Integrate SOP-CLIN-002 (Risk Management)** into the validation workflow with an explicit gate, rather than treating it as a separate, referenced document.
4. **Add an Article 73 serious incident reporting procedure** with defined timelines and reporting paths to EU market surveillance authorities.
5. **Expand the RACI matrix** (Section 3) into a full accountability framework covering all Article 17(1) aspects.
6. **Add a conformity assessment strategy** section specifying which Article 43 procedure applies and how modifications trigger re-assessment.
7. **Add a technical documentation maintenance procedure** requiring updates upon any material change, not just on the annual review cycle.

Compliance level: gap
